EDCS-12401642.summary: This inscription records a filial memorial commissioned by two sons for their deceased father, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-12401653.translation: To Gaius Julius Caesar, son of Gaius, emperor, triumvir for restoring the republic, patron, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-12401653.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Gaius Julius Caesar, identifying him by his full name, multiple high offices, and acknowledging him as a patron, likely dating to the late Republic or early Principate.

EDCS-12401658.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Agileia Severa lived 30 years, 20 days, and 2 hours. Hermes [erected this] to one well-deserving.
EDCS-12401658.summary: This funerary inscription records the age at death of Agileia Severa and indicates it was erected by Hermes, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401659.translation: Claudia Valentina made this for her husband Claudius Eutychianus, who lived 47 years, 5 months, with whom she lived 15 years, 5 months.
EDCS-12401659.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages and duration of marriage for Claudia Valentina and her husband Claudius Eutychianus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401660.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the underworld. To my daughter, daughter of Gaius, the Pious Filiae, Felicula. Publius Tettius Secundinus made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-12401660.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by Publius Tettius Secundinus to his deceased wife Felicula, identifying her parentage and describing her as 'Pia', likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401661.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Quintus Marcius Glyptianus and Marcia Eutychia.
EDCS-12401661.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the underworld spirits for Quintus Marcius Glyptianus and Marcia Eutychia, likely a joint funerary monument from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401662.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the underworld. He made this for his well-deserving husband.
EDCS-12401662.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication to the underworld spirits for Numerius Oppius Saturninus, commissioned by his spouse, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401663.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the underworld. He lived one year and 11 days. Marcus Petronius Suavis and Sextilia Lucretia made this for their most dutiful son.
EDCS-12401663.summary: This funerary inscription records the short life of Marcus Petronius Laicus, who lived just over one year, and was commissioned by his parents, Marcus Petronius Suavis and Sextilia Lucretia, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401664.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Pompeia Pollitta lived 60 years, 3 months, and 5 days, and 2 hours. Her husband made this for one well-deserving.
EDCS-12401664.summary: This funerary inscription records the age at death of Pompeia Pollitta and indicates it was erected by her husband, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401666.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Publicius Priscinus, an unhappy father, made this for his most beloved son, Quintus Publicius Ianurius, son of Quintus, who lived 33 years, 5 months, and 28 days.
EDCS-12401666.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Publicius Ianurius, who died at the age of 33, erected by his father.

EDCS-12401667.translation: Publius Sextilius Felix, freedman of Publius, made this for himself and for Sextilia Nobilis, freedwoman of Publius, his wife. This monument or tomb will not follow the heir.
EDCS-12401667.summary: This is a funerary monument for Publius Sextilius Felix and his wife, indicating it is not inheritable.

EDCS-12401668.translation: His father made this for Aliarius, his incomparable younger son, who lived 22 years, 10 months, and 8 days.
EDCS-12401668.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a son named Aliarius, erected by his father.

EDCS-12401679.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus \n
EDCS-12401679.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-12401679.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.

EDCS-12401687.translation: Lucius Scribonius Libo, son of Lucius and patron, dedicated the basilica from his own funds.
EDCS-12401687.summary: This is an inscription dedicating a basilica built by Lucius Scribonius Libo, son of Lucius.

EDCS-12700146.text_edition: \n Caio Umbrio Apolausto decurioni et quaestori Beneventano Caius Umbrius Rufinus filius et Titus Cominius Felicissimus frater bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-12700146.translation: His son Caius Umbrius Rufinus and his brother Titus Cominius Felicissimus made this for Caius Umbrius Apolaustus, decurion and quaestor of Beneventum, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-12700146.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a son and brother for Caius Umbrius Apolaustus, an official in Beneventum.

EDCS-12700217.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae viam et pontes a Benevento Brundisium pecunia sua fecit\n
EDCS-12700217.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, Imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, made the road and bridges from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-12700217.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's construction of a road and bridges from Beneventum to Brundisium, funded by himself.

EDCS-12800112.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus consul VII fines agrorum publicorum municipii Canusini ex formis publicis restituit\n
EDCS-12800112.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, in his 7th consulship, restored the boundaries of the public lands of the municipality of Canusium according to public records.
EDCS-12800112.summary: This inscription records Emperor Vespasian restoring public land boundaries in Canusium.

EDCS-12800115.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Antonino Caesari imperatori destinato Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici pontificis maximi fortissimi ac maximi et super omnis providentissimi principis filio voto numini Minervae suscepto ordo populusque Canusinus\n
EDCS-12800115.translation: The order and people of Canusium dedicated [this to] Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, designated emperor, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, pontifex maximus, most brave and great, most provident prince above all, in fulfillment of a vow to the divinity of Minerva.
EDCS-12800115.summary: This is a dedication by the citizens of Canusium to Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, heir-designate of Septimius Severus, in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-12800118.text_edition: \n Marco Arrio Clementiano viro clarissimo ob insignem benevolentiae sic eius erga cives et patriam ordo et populus Canusinus decurionum decreto dedicavit\n
EDCS-12800118.translation: The order and people of Canusium dedicated this by decree of the decurions to Marcus Arrius Clementianus, a most distinguished man, for his outstanding benevolence towards the citizens and the fatherland.
EDCS-12800118.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the citizens of Canusium to Marcus Arrius Clementianus for his benevolence.

EDCS-12800120.text_edition: \n Sacrum Dis Manibus L(ucius) Baebius Luci filius Oufentina Strabo filius tribunus militum legionis III bis auratae fecit\n
EDCS-12800120.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Baebius Strabo, son of Lucius, of the Oufentina tribe, son of Lucius, tribune of the soldiers of the IIIrd Ala Aurata, made (this).
EDCS-12800120.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the deceased by Lucius Baebius Strabo, a military tribune, likely for himself or a family member.

EDCS-12800121.translation: Clodius, tribune of the soldiers, former centurion of the first rank.
EDCS-12800121.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Clodius as a military officer with prior experience as a primipilaris.

EDCS-12800122.text_edition: \n Caio Aemilio Cai filio Trachalo veterano legionis III Gallicae et Aemiliis Frontoni et Miccioni et Trachalae filis et coniugi ia Miccia fecit\n
EDCS-12800122.translation: Aemilia Miccia made (this) for Gaius Aemilius Trachalus, son of Gaius, a veteran of the IIIrd Gallic Legion, and for her sons Aemilius Fronton and Miccion and Trachala, and for her husband.
EDCS-12800122.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Aemilia Miccia for her veteran husband, Gaius Aemilius Trachalus, and other family members.

EDCS-12800123.text_edition: \n Cordio Publi filio Flacco publice municipium ex senatus consulto\n
EDCS-12800123.translation: The municipality, by a decree of the senate, (made this) for Cordius Flaccus, son of Publius.
EDCS-12800123.summary: This inscription records a public dedication by the municipality to Cordius Flaccus, son of Publius, ordered by the senate.

EDCS-12800124.translation: Ennius, son of Gaius, a duovir.
EDCS-12800124.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying Ennius, son of Gaius, as a duovir, a type of local magistrate.

EDCS-12800125.text_edition: \n illius Valerianus IIIIvir aedilis IIIIvir iure dicundo praefectus sibi et o Eroti Augustali ae Sophe uxori eius ae Quetae Tampiae ae uxoribus suis\n
EDCS-12800125.translation: Valerianus, son of Illius, duovir, aedile, duovir for administering justice, prefect, (made this) for himself and for Erotius Augustalis, his wife Sophe, his wife Queta Tampia, and his wives.
EDCS-12800125.summary: This is a funerary or dedication inscription made by Valerianus, a prominent local official, for himself and several female relatives including wives and possibly slaves or freedwomen.

EDCS-12800129.text_edition: \n Lucius Annius Luci libertus Asbestus Augustalis Caeliae |(mulieris) libertae Euposiae Euaristo liberto Onesimo liberto Asbesto Melete\n
EDCS-12800129.translation: Lucius Annius Asbestus, freedman of Lucius, Augustalis, (made this) for Caelia Euposia, freedwoman of Caelia, and for the freedman Onesimus, and for the freedman Asbestus, and for Melete.
EDCS-12800129.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Lucius Annius Asbestus, a freedman and Augustalis, for his freedwoman Caelia Euposia and other freedmen and a woman named Melete.

EDCS-12800130.text_edition: \n Publio Poppaedio Publi liberto Secundo Augustali Poppaediae Pudori libertae PPubliis Poppaedis filis\n
EDCS-12800130.translation: For Publius Poppaedius Secundus, freedman of Publius, Augustalis, and for Poppaedia Pudoris, freedwoman of Publius, and for the sons of Publius Poppaedius.
EDCS-12800130.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Publius Poppaedius Secundus, a freedman and Augustalis, and members of the Poppaedius family.

EDCS-12800132.text_edition: \n Phileros Augustalis Agathemerini et Lucio io Luci liberto Zosimo arbitratu Auli Pi \n
EDCS-12800132.translation: Phileros Augustalis, (made this) for Agathemerinus and for Lucius Zosimus, freedman of Lucius, by the arbitration of Aulus Pi.
EDCS-12800132.summary: This inscription records a transaction or dedication made by Phileros Augustalis, involving Agathemerinus and Lucius Zosimus, under the supervision of Aulus Pi.

EDCS-12800133.text_edition: \n Publio Fabricio Publi liberto Eroti magistro Augustali Valeriae Publi libertae Quartae Publio Fabricio Publi liberto Vero vixit nnos V Fabriciae Publi libertae Verae matri Romaniae Cai libertae Trypherae Publius Fabricius Cladus magister Augustali fecit\n
EDCS-12800133.translation: Publius Fabricius Cladus, magister Augustalis, made (this) for Publius Fabricius Eros, freedman of Publius, magistro Augustalis, for Valeria Quartae, freedwoman of Publius, for Publius Fabricius Verus, son of Publius Fabricius, who lived 5 years, and for Fabricia Verae, freedwoman of Publius, and for Romaniae Tryphera, freedwoman of Caius.
EDCS-12800133.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Publius Fabricius Cladus, a magister Augustalis, for his family members, including his son Publius Fabricius Verus who died at age five, and various freedmen and freedwomen.

EDCS-12800137.text_edition: \n Albia Quinti liberta |(mulieris) liberta Fausta Quintus Albius Quinti libertus Clarus fecit suis et Mettia Luci liberta Plusia sita\n
EDCS-12800137.translation: Fausta, freedwoman of Albius Clarus, freedman of Quintus, and Plusia, freedwoman of Lucius Mettius, made this for themselves and their freedpersons.
EDCS-12800137.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by two freedwomen for themselves and their dependents.

EDCS-12800241.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription mentioning a magistrate named Phila, likely related to a collegium.

EDCS-12800242.text_edition: \n Caius Pomponius Forensis magister Mercurialis vixit annos XL hic situs est\n
EDCS-12800242.translation: Caius Pomponius Forensis, magistrate of the Mercuriales, lived 40 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-12800242.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Pomponius Forensis, who served as a magistrate of the collegium Mercurialis and died at age 40.

EDCS-12800244.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Eros publicus victimarius vixit annos XL collegius sic de suo privatim\n
EDCS-12800244.translation: To the Divine Manes. Eros, the public victimarius, lived 40 years; the collegium, privately, from their own funds.
EDCS-12800244.summary: This funerary inscription is for Eros, a public victimarius, and was funded by his collegium.

EDCS-12800245.text_edition: \n Tiberius Iulius tribu Palatina Aeneas Vibone comparavit sibi et suis in nnos C\n
EDCS-12800245.translation: Tiberius Iulius Aeneas, of the Palatine tribe, purchased this place for himself and his family for 100 years.
EDCS-12800245.summary: This is a purchase record for burial plots by Tiberius Iulius Aeneas for himself and his family, valid for 100 years.

EDCS-12800246.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Felix publicus Brundisinorum servus aquarius vixit annos XXXVIII\n
EDCS-12800246.translation: To the Divine Manes. Felix, public slave of the Brundisines, the water carrier, lived 38 years.
EDCS-12800246.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Felix, a public slave and water carrier for the people of Brundisium, who died at age 38.

EDCS-12800521.text_edition: \n e Cai libertae Amoebeni vixit nnos bene merenti misera mater posuit filiae\n
EDCS-12800521.translation: To Amoebeni, freedwoman of Caius, who lived well. Her distressed mother placed this for her deserving daughter.
EDCS-12800521.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a mother for her deserving freedwoman daughter.

EDCS-12800523.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Arruntio Asiatico vixit nnnos XXII Marcus Arruntius Issus et Arruntia Asia parentes filio bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-12800523.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Arruntius Issus and Arruntia Asia, parents, made this for their well-deserving son, Marcus Arruntius Asiaticus, who lived 22 years.
EDCS-12800523.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Arruntius Asiaticus, erected by his parents.

EDCS-12800527.translation: Baebia, daughter of Publius, [---] for herself and for her son Lucius [---] and daughter Polla [---]
EDCS-12800527.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription indicating Baebia is dedicating a tomb for herself and her children.

EDCS-12800528.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Baebiae Quinti filiae Rufinae\n
EDCS-12800528.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes of Baebia Rufina, daughter of Quintus.
EDCS-12800528.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Baebia Rufina.

EDCS-12800529.translation: For Baebia and for Aulus Chrys, for themselves.
EDCS-12800529.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Baebia and Aulus Chrys, likely set up by one of them for themselves.

EDCS-12800531.summary: This inscription is a dedication, likely funerary, for a person named Campila Glyconis.

EDCS-12800532.text_edition: \n ? Carvili Luci libert-- sibi et Lucio Carvilio Basso patrono suo et Lucio Carvilio Luci filio Celeri filio suo\n
EDCS-12800532.translation: For myself and for Lucius Carvilius Bassus, my patron, and for my son, Lucius Carvilius Celer, son of Lucius Carvilius.
EDCS-12800532.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by a freedman of the Carvilii family for himself, his patron, and his own son.

EDCS-12800534.text_edition: \n Publius Chrysius Publi libertus Faustio sibi et Herenniae |(mulieris) libertae Helpini uxori suae\n
EDCS-12800534.translation: For myself and for Herennia Helpis, my wife, my freedwoman.
EDCS-12800534.summary: This funerary inscription was set up by Publius Chrysius Faustus, a freedman, for himself and his wife, Herennia Helpis.

EDCS-12800539.text_edition: \n Sextus Deccianus Sexti libertus Pothus or sibi et Deccianae Urbanae libertae suae hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-12800539.translation: Sextus Deccianus Pothus, freedman of Sextus, for himself and for Decciana Urbana, his freedwoman, his heir shall not follow this monument.
EDCS-12800539.summary: This funerary inscription states that the heir of Sextus Deccianus Pothus and his freedwoman Decciana Urbana will not inherit or be responsible for this monument.

EDCS-12800543.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Flavia Duta.

EDCS-12800544.text_edition: \n Flaviae Valentinae coniugi sanctissimae Antonius Vindex bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-12800544.translation: Antonius Vindex made this for his most virtuous wife, Flavia Valentina, who deserved well.
EDCS-12800544.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Antonius Vindex to his deserving and virtuous wife, Flavia Valentina.

EDCS-12800546.text_edition: \n Caio Galbio Pisto Galleia uxor Dasimia\n
EDCS-12800546.translation: To Gaius Galbius Pisto. His wife Dasimia Galleia.
EDCS-12800546.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Gaius Galbius Pisto by his wife, Dasimia Galleia.

EDCS-12800549.translation: Aqustinus, laid here, [---] with his wife Iulia Chrysaris.
EDCS-12800549.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Aqustinus and his wife Iulia Chrysaris, with part of the text lost.

EDCS-12800550.text_edition: \n Ddis Mmanibus contra botum sic donantis et scribentis Iuliis Pacato Senecioni primariis civitatis suae omni semper laude et honorificentia dignissimis quorum meritum et testimonium provinciae notum est Antistia Sapidia optima nurus et coniux fecit sibique simul\n
EDCS-12800550.translation: To the Divine Manes. Against the vow, thus giving and writing: To the Juliis Pacatus Senecio, foremost men of their city, always most worthy of all praise and honor, whose merit and testimony are known to the province, Antistia Sapidia, their best daughter-in-law and wife, made this, and for herself at the same time.
EDCS-12800550.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Antistia Sapidia to the Divine Manes of her husband and father-in-law, recognizing their distinguished civic service.

EDCS-12800553.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius [---] Zosimus(?).
EDCS-12800553.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the departed, likely for Publius and Zosimus.

EDCS-12800554.text_edition: \n Sextus Mutronius Quinti filius Sacie<r=D>dos sic Lollia Marci filia Rufa sitei sic \n
EDCS-12800554.translation: Sextus Mutronius, son of Quintus, called Sacredos, and Lollia Rufa, daughter of Marcus, are here.
EDCS-12800554.summary: This funerary inscription identifies Sextus Mutronius and Lollia Rufa, placing them at this location.

EDCS-12800555.text_edition: \n Publius Pacilius |(mulieris) libertus Agilis sibi et Paciliae |(mulieris) libertae Antiochini et Publio Pacilio Publi liberto Expectato\n
EDCS-12800555.translation: Publius Pacilius Agilis, freedman of a woman, made this for himself and for Pacilia Antiochinus, freedwoman of a woman, and for Publius Pacilius Expectatus, freedman of Publius.
EDCS-12800555.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Publius Pacilius Agilis for himself and for his freedwoman and freedman, as well as a freedman of Publius.

EDCS-12800556.translation: [---] made for the preserved [---] and for his deserving son Primigenius and for the most dutiful slave Suavis.
EDCS-12800556.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by an unknown person for a freedwoman, their son Primigenius, and a slave named Suavis.

EDCS-12800557.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Primo actori Iuni Rustici\n
EDCS-12800557.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Primo, the actor of Iunius Rusticus.
EDCS-12800557.summary: A funerary dedication to the spirits of the departed for Primo, an actor employed by Iunius Rusticus.

EDCS-12800559.text_edition: \n Lucius Sabellius |(mulieris) libertus Primus Furfania |(mulieris) liberta Tertia usor sic\n
EDCS-12800559.translation: Lucius Sabellius Primus, freedman of a woman, and Furfania Tertia, freedwoman of a woman, are here.
EDCS-12800559.summary: This funerary inscription identifies Lucius Sabellius Primus and Furfania Tertia as being interred here.

EDCS-12800561.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Servilio Crispino menses XX dies XIX Crispina filio dulcissimo\n
EDCS-12800561.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Servilius Crispinus, aged 20 months and 19 days, dearest son of Crispina.
EDCS-12800561.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the departed for Gaius Servilius Crispinus, a young child, by his mother Crispina.

EDCS-12800562.text_edition: \n Socelius Iustus Soceliae Priscillae matri suae bene merenti fecit vixit annis LX\n
EDCS-12800562.translation: Socelius Iustus made this for his mother Socelia Priscilla, well deserving, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-12800562.summary: This epitaph records that Socelius Iustus erected a monument for his mother, Socelia Priscilla, who lived to be 60 years old.

EDCS-12800563.text_edition: \n Tampia Cai liberta Hilara fecit sibi et Caio Tampio Cai liberto Eroti patrono et Caio Tampio Cai liberto Chaeriae colliberto\n
EDCS-12800563.translation: Tampia Hilara, freedwoman of Gaius, made this for herself and for Gaius Tampius Eros, freedman of Gaius, her patron, and for Gaius Tampius Chaeriae, fellow freedman.
EDCS-12800563.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Tampia Hilara, a freedwoman, for herself, her patron Gaius Tampius Eros, and a fellow freedman.

EDCS-12800564.text_edition: \n Caius Tettidius Tettidia Luci liberta A Caius Tettidius Marcus Clodius Marci libertus? Clodia Marci liberta? \n
EDCS-12800564.translation: Gaius Tettidius, freedman of Lucia, daughter of Tettidia Lucius. Gaius Tettidius. Marcus Clodius, freedman of Marcus? Clodia, freedwoman of Marcus?
EDCS-12800564.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to list several individuals, possibly related, including members of the Tettidius and Clodius families, with uncertain relationships and status.

EDCS-12800569.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Salluviae Ceriali vixit annis XXIIII Marcus Ulpius Augusti libertus Tiro uxori bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-12800569.translation: To the divine spirits. Salluvia Cerialis lived for 24 years. Marcus Ulpius Tiro, freedman of Augustus, erected this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-12800569.summary: A funerary epitaph for Salluvia Cerialis, erected by her husband.

EDCS-12800578.translation: Aulus?, from the Oufentina tribe, Ma[...
EDCS-12800578.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly a personal name and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-12800599.text_edition: \n LXXV Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator VI consul pater patriae viam a Benevento Brundisium pecunia sua fecit\n
EDCS-12800599.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, in his 13th year of tribunician power, 6th acclamation as emperor, father of the country, built the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-12800599.summary: An imperial dedication by Emperor Trajan for the construction of a road.

EDCS-12800601.text_edition: \n Dddominis nnnostris Flavio Crispo Valerio Liciniano Licinio iuniori Flavio Valerio Constantino nobbbilissimis Caesssaribus\n
EDCS-12800601.translation: To our lords, Flavius Crispus Valerius Licinianus Licinius the Younger, Flavius Valerius Constantinus, the most noble Caesars.
EDCS-12800601.summary: A dedication to the emperors Crispus and Constantine, likely from the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-12800603.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae viam a Benevento Brundisium pecunia sua fecit\n
EDCS-12800603.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, in his 13th year of tribunician power, 6th acclamation as emperor, father of the country, built the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-12800603.summary: An imperial dedication by Emperor Trajan for the construction of a road.

EDCS-12800605.text_edition: \n Dominorum nostrorum Diocletiani et Maximiani Auggustorum et Constanti et Maximiani Caesarum \n
EDCS-12800605.translation: To our lords Diocletian and Maximian Augusti and Constantius and Maximian Caesars.
EDCS-12800605.summary: A dedication to the emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Maximian, likely from the Tetrarchic period.

EDCS-12800606.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, Flavius Valerius Constantinus, son of the divine Constantius, pious, fortunate, invincible Augustus, consul for the 3rd time, acclaimed emperor for the 9th time, father of the country, proconsul. [Dedicated] to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-12800606.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Constantine I, mentioning his titles and a dedication to Jupiter.

EDCS-12800607.text_edition: \n Marco Aquilio Marci filio Fabia Roma Felici procuratori a censibus equitum Romanorum praefecto classis praetoriae nnis procuratori rationis patrimonii procuratori operum publicorum et fiscalium urbis sacrae procuratori rationis patrimonii iterum procuratori rationis privatae Augusti nostri praeposito vexillationis agentium in Italia pontifici coloniae Lanuvinorum praeposito legionis XI Claudiae patrono municipii multis meritisque eius publice decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-12800607.translation: To Marcus Aquilius Felix, son of Marcus, of the Fabia tribe, from Rome, procurator of Roman knights in the census, prefect of the praetorian fleet, procurator of the accounts of the patrimony, procurator of public and fiscal works of the sacred city, procurator of the accounts of the patrimony again, procurator of the private accounts of our Augustus, overseer of the detachment of agents in Italy, pontiff of the colony of Lanuvium, overseer of the legion XI Claudia, patron of the municipality, [honored] for his many merits and services by public decree of the decurions.
EDCS-12800607.summary: A lengthy honorary inscription for Marcus Aquilius Felix, detailing his numerous administrative and military posts in the Roman Empire.

EDCS-12800610.translation: Cosmus [for] himself and Prima [his] freedwoman [purchased] a plot [of land] 12 feet in front and 12 feet in depth.
EDCS-12800610.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the purchase of a burial plot for Cosmus and his freedwoman Prima.

EDCS-12800611.text_edition: \n Publius Valerius Publi libertus Daphnus fecit sibi et suis Valeria Publi liberta Secunda Publius Valerius Publi libertus Felix\n
EDCS-12800611.translation: Publius Valerius Daphnus, freedman of Publius, made [this] for himself and his family. Valeria Secunda, freedwoman of Publius. Publius Valerius Felix, freedman of Publius.
EDCS-12800611.summary: A funerary epitaph for multiple individuals, likely family members, erected by Publius Valerius Daphnus.

EDCS-12800617.translation: To the departed spirits. Libuscidia Vitalini lived 11 years. Publius Libuscidius Felissimus made this for his well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-12800617.summary: A tombstone erected by Publius Libuscidius Felissimus for his daughter Libuscidia Vitalini, who died at the age of 11.

EDCS-12800619.text_edition: \n Lucius Tullius Luci filius Nasica Tullia Luci liberta Salvia Lucius Tullius Luci filius Naso Tutoria Publi liberta Phronesis uxor\n
EDCS-12800619.translation: Lucius Tullius Nasica, son of Lucius Tullius; Salvia, freedwoman of Lucius Tullius; Naso, son of Lucius Tullius; Tutoria Phronesis, freedwoman of Publius, wife.
EDCS-12800619.summary: A list of names, possibly from a tombstone or legal document, including freedmen and a wife, related to the Tullii family.

EDCS-12800620.text_edition: \n Caius Caesellius Eros sibi et patrono Caio Caesellio Cai filio Gemello et Ataniae |(mulieris) libertae Apheliae de suo\n
EDCS-12800620.translation: Gaius Caesellius Eros for himself, and for his patron Gaius Caesellius Gemellus, son of Gaius, and for his freedwoman Atania Aphelia, from his own funds.
EDCS-12800620.summary: An inscription recording a dedication made by Gaius Caesellius Eros for himself, his patron, and his freedwoman.

EDCS-12800745.text_edition: \n Balineum publicum a Caesidio Proculo refectum res publica vetustate quassatum decurionum decreto atplicito sic omni cultu restituit\n
EDCS-12800745.translation: The public bath, having been damaged by age, was restored by Caesidius Proculus; by decree of the decurions, he applied it with all care and restored it.
EDCS-12800745.summary: An inscription documenting the repair and restoration of a public bathhouse by Caesidius Proculus, commissioned by the town council.

EDCS-12800748.translation: To Dastidia Napae, freedwoman of Publius, mother; to Dastidia Prisca, daughter of Titus, who lived 15 years; to Etria Zosima; to Etria Peregrina; to Aulus Dastidius Plocamus, freedman of [the woman].
EDCS-12800748.summary: A tombstone commemorating several individuals, including a mother, her daughter who died at 15, and other family members, likely from the Dastidii and Etriae families.

EDCS-12800752.translation: Marcus Laberius, son of Marcus, of the Galeria tribe.
EDCS-12800752.summary: A name with tribal affiliation, likely from a tombstone or public record, identifying Marcus Laberius.

EDCS-12800754.text_edition: \n Magnio Fusco Pompulliae Salviae Magni duo fratres parentibus\n
EDCS-12800754.translation: Magnus and his two brothers [made this] for their parents, Pompullia Salvia and Magnus.
EDCS-12800754.summary: A dedication by two brothers for their parents, Pompullia Salvia and Magnus.

EDCS-12800755.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Protho vixit annos XIIII menses III\n
EDCS-12800755.translation: To the departed spirits. Protho lived 14 years and 3 months.
EDCS-12800755.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Protho at the age of 14 years and 3 months.

EDCS-12800931.translation: For the public affairs of the citizens of Canusium, under the supervision of Lucius Eggius Marullus.
EDCS-12800931.summary: An inscription indicating public works or administration in the town of Canusium, overseen by Lucius Eggius Marullus.

EDCS-12800932.translation: For the public affairs of Canusium, managed by Publius Graecidius Firmus.
EDCS-12800932.summary: An inscription indicating public administration in Canusium, managed by Publius Graecidius Firmus.

EDCS-12800934.translation: Under the supervision of Publius Graecidius Firmus, on behalf of the public affairs of the people of Canusium.
EDCS-12800934.summary: This is an inscription concerning public works in Canusium, managed by Publius Graecidius Firmus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13302447.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Purpurio retiarius Graecus ad Caesaris ludum magnum MOADV occidit pugna XI bene merenti conservi fecerunt Dis Manibus Filematio natus Agrippinae qui vixit anos XXX retiarius pugnarum XV Aurelia Afrhodite sic bene merenti fecit coniugi dulcissimo a<d=Q> ludum magnum Caesaris \n
EDCS-13302447.translation: To the Divine Shades. Purpurius, the Greek retiarius of Caesar's great ludus, died in battle on the 11th day. His fellow gladiators, wishing him well, erected this. To the Divine Shades. Filematus, son of Agrippina, who lived 30 years, a retiarius of 15 battles. Aurelia Aphrodite erected this for her most beloved husband, a gladiator of Caesar's great ludus.
EDCS-13302447.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates two gladiators, Purpurius and Filematus, who died in the arena, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-13500261.text_edition: \n Sexto Silettio Sexti filio Camilia Maximo IIIIviro iure dicundo decreto decurionum Sextus Silettius Epitunchanus sic et Vipstana Helice parentes honore accepto impensam remiserunt et dedicatione eius decurionibus HS XII dedit item municipibus et incolis HS IIII nummum\n
EDCS-13500261.translation: For Sextus Silettius Maximus, son of Sextus Silettius, Camilia tribe, quadruvir for dispensing justice, by decree of the decurions. Sextus Silettius Epitunchanus and Vipstana Helice, parents, having received the honor, remitted the expense, and gave 12,000 sesterces to the decurions for its dedication; likewise 4,000 nummi to the townspeople and residents.
EDCS-13500261.summary: This inscription honors Sextus Silettius Maximus, a local magistrate, and records the family's remission of expenses and their subsequent donations to the community, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13500263.text_edition: \n Lucio Arrio Luci filio Fabia Proculo pontifici \n
EDCS-13500263.translation: For Lucius Arrius Proculus, son of Lucius, Fabia tribe, priest.
EDCS-13500263.summary: This is a dedication for Lucius Arrius Proculus, a priest of the Fabia tribe, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13900311.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Savonia, daughter of Numerius Savonius, of the Nevia tribe. Antonius Fortunatus erected this for his most holy wife, with whom he lived 20 years, 7 months, 22 days, and 10 hours.
EDCS-13900311.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a husband for his wife, providing her age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13900312.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. For Alexander, son of Zenodoros, of Flavia. Marcella, his sister, placed this.
EDCS-13900312.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a sister for her deceased brother, Alexander, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13900319.translation: [For] Anepte. [---] Secunda. [For her freedman?] and [For Aphrodisius, her patron], son of Lucius. [For his] wife.
EDCS-13900319.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription that mentions several individuals, including a freedman and a patron, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13900321.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Tiberius Claudius Eutychus, his well-deserving husband. Claudia Filete, freedwoman of Tiberius, and for herself, her freedmen and freedwomen, and for Publius Graecidius Thallus and their descendants.
EDCS-13900321.summary: This funerary inscription honors a husband and also provides for the deceased's own freedmen and descendants, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13900322.translation: For Lucius Annianus Pamphilus Primus, freedman of Lucius. His freedmen placed this.
EDCS-13900322.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by freedmen for their former master, Lucius Annianus Pamphilus Primus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13900323.translation: For Catallage, wife of Camillus Rutilius, a wool-weigher. Primus Rutilius, his manager, placed this for his fellow servant and for himself.
EDCS-13900323.summary: This funerary inscription honors Catallage, wife of a wool-weigher, and was erected by her husband's manager for her and himself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13900324.translation: Marcia Epicharis, daughter of Ge (?), placed here.
EDCS-13900324.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Marcia Epicharis for herself or a relative.

EDCS-13900325.translation: Lucius Decimius placed [this] for Mariae Nemusae, his deserving fellow soldier.
EDCS-13900325.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Lucius Decimius to his deserving fellow soldier, Mariae Nemusae.

EDCS-13900326.translation: Bruttius Catus, her husband, placed [this] for his incomparable and most chaste wife, Pomponia Aphrodisia, daughter of Lucius, who lived 24 years.
EDCS-13900326.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Bruttius Catus for his wife Pomponia Aphrodisia, detailing her lifespan.

EDCS-13900327.translation: Firvia Dioclia, freedwoman of a woman, placed here. Publius Stlaccius Athenio, freedman of Publius, [placed this].
EDCS-13900327.summary: A funerary epitaph for Firvia Dioclia, set up by Publius Stlaccius Athenio.

EDCS-13900328.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. [Dedicated] for Sergia Chryseis, his friend.
EDCS-13900328.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Sergia Chryseis, identified as a friend.

EDCS-13900331.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Salutaris lived 35 years. Diadochus [placed this] for his deserving fellow servant.
EDCS-13900331.summary: A funerary epitaph for Salutaris, who lived 35 years, erected by his fellow servant Diadochus.

EDCS-13900332.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Octavia M[---]. Phoebus [placed this?].
EDCS-13900332.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph mentioning Octavia and possibly Phoebus.

EDCS-13900333.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Eufrosyne lived 2 years and 6 months. Nicephoris made [this] for her most sweet foster-child, for herself and her freedmen.
EDCS-13900333.summary: A funerary epitaph for Eufrosyne, who lived 2 years and 6 months, made by Nicephoris for her foster-child and potentially for herself and freedmen.

EDCS-13900335.translation: Lucius Servilius, son of Gnaeus, is buried here.
EDCS-13900335.summary: A funerary epitaph identifying Lucius Servilius as the deceased.

EDCS-14803828.translation: Stephani the overseer is buried here.
EDCS-14803828.summary: A funerary epitaph stating that Stephani, a vilicus (overseer), is buried here.

EDCS-14804837.translation: To the departed spirits. Mammius Mammiamus, a praetorian soldier, who lived for 10 years and more, and Donatus, his wet nurse, were placed here by their mother, Hilara, and their brother, Surio Frontoniano, who lived for 3 years and more.
EDCS-14804837.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Surio Frontoniano, Mammius Mammiamus, and Donatus, placed by their mother and brother.

EDCS-14804844.translation: To Pacedia, daughter of Sextus Cogitatus. Know that you are a human and know that you must die. Farewell.
EDCS-14804844.summary: This is a short, epigrammatic epitaph for Pacedia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15300296.text_edition: \n Lucius Scultidius Sexti filius Pollia Rufus veteranus legionis VIII Augustae eques militavit annos XXVI vixi<t=I> annos VL hic situs est heres faciendum quravit sic \n
EDCS-15300296.translation: Lucius Scultidius Rufus, son of Sextus, of the Pollia tribe, a veteran of the Legio VIII Augusta, served for 26 years and lived for 54 years. He is buried here. His heir had this made.
EDCS-15300296.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Scultidius Rufus, a veteran of Legio VIII Augusta, detailing his military service and lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15500032.text_edition: \n Marcus Serveius Treptus Silvano votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15500032.translation: Marcus Serveius Treptus willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-15500032.summary: This is a dedication of a votive offering to the god Silvanus by Marcus Serveius Treptus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100407.text_edition: \n X Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII consul III viam Appiam per millia passus XVDCCL longa vetustate amissam adiectis HS MMMMMMMMMLVIInovem quinquaginta septem milibus ad HS CCCCCLXIXCquinquagentia undeseptuaginta milia centum quae possessores agrorum contulerunt fecit CLXXIIII\n
EDCS-16100407.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajanus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, made the Appian Way, 15,270 Roman miles long, which had been lost due to age, with an additional 8,577,570 sesterces from the landowners of the fields, at a cost of 5,670,070 sesterces. This inscription was set up by the builders (CLXXIIII).
EDCS-16100407.summary: This is an official inscription by Emperor Hadrian recording the repair and rebuilding of the Appian Way, funded by public and private contributions, dating to his 7th tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-16100408.text_edition: \n Caio Umbricio Cai filio Scaptia Meliori haruspici Caesarum patrono municipi ex testamento ipsius\n
EDCS-16100408.translation: To Gaius Umbricius Melior, son of Gaius, of the Scaptia tribe, an haruspex and patron of the municipality, from his will.
EDCS-16100408.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Umbricius Melior, a haruspex and municipal patron, established posthumously according to his will, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200940.text_edition: \n Oppius Athenio Settino Silvano votum libens solvit Centiano sic et Basso consulibus Kalendis Septembribus\n
EDCS-16200940.translation: Oppius Athenio willingly paid his vow to Settinius Silvanus. This was done during the consulship of Centianus and Bassus, on the Kalends of September.
EDCS-16200940.summary: This is a votive dedication to Settinius Silvanus by Oppius Athenio, dated by the consuls Centianus and Bassus to September 1, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200941.text_edition: \n Lucius Fufius Publi libertus ricae proc c s magistri collegii restituerunt\n
EDCS-16200941.translation: The magistrates of the college restored this. Lucius Fufius, freedman of Publius, and Rica, the curator.
EDCS-16200941.summary: This inscription records the restoration of something by the magistrates of a college, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200942.text_edition: \n Collegium tibicinum Matris Deum Idaeae magistri curaverunt Marcus Sabidius Marci libertus Caius Atilius Cai libertus Cnaeus Egnati Cnaei servus nnus Luci servus\n
EDCS-16200942.translation: The college of pipers of the Mother Goddess of Idaea, the magistrates saw to this: Marcus Sabidius, freedman of Marcus; Gaius Atilius, freedman of Gaius; Cnaeus Egnatius, slave of Cnaeus; Servus, slave of Lucius.
EDCS-16200942.summary: This inscription records the work done by the magistrates of a college of pipers dedicated to the Mother Goddess of Idaea, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200943.text_edition: \n Sacrum Silvano Corneliano permissu Cai Luci Nei Rufini Pamphilus Rufinus salus PMPRDBR maius\n
EDCS-16200943.translation: Sacred offering to Silvanus Cornelianus, by permission of Gaius Lucius Neius Rufus. Pamphilus Rufus, good health and well-being.
EDCS-16200943.summary: This is a sacred offering to Silvanus Cornelianus by Pamphilus Rufus, with permission from Gaius Lucius Neius Rufus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16300024.text_edition: \n Acesi Honorati P C Lucio Staio Luci filio Stellatina Scrateio Maniliano praetori Ceriali iure dicundo quinquennali filio equitis Romani archiatri Benevento hic primus ob honorem cerialitatis tesseris sparsis in aurum argentum aes vestem lentiamen ceteraque populo divisit Lucius Staius Eutychus avus et Tatiana avia locus datus decreto decurionum Pompeiano et Paeligniano consulibus\n
EDCS-16300024.translation: To Acesi Honorati, son of Publius, from the Stellatina tribe, to Lucius Staius Scrateius Manilianus, son of Lucius, praetor, Cerialis, holding the office of iuris dicundi for five years, son of an equestrian, chief physician. In Benevento, Lucius Staius Eutychus, his grandfather, and Tatiana, his grandmother, gave him land, by decree of the decurions, with Pompeianus and Paelignianus as consuls. He was the first for the office of Cerialis to distribute tesserae (tokens) made of gold, silver, bronze, clothing, lentils, and other items to the people.
EDCS-16300024.summary: This inscription records a grant of land and a public distribution of goods by a grandfather and grandmother in honor of their grandson's achievement of a public office in Benevento, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16300405.text_edition: \n Caio L Praefecto magistro Mercuriali Arescusa et L Corinthus magister Mercurialis bene merito L Festo liberto\n
EDCS-16300405.translation: To Gaius Lucius, Prefect, Master of the Mercurial guild, from Arescusa and Lucius Corinthus, Masters of the Mercurial guild, for a well-deserving freedman, Lucius Festus.
EDCS-16300405.summary: This is a dedication by guild masters to a deserving freedman, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16400385.text_edition: \n Clodiae Luci filiae Anthianillae Marci Coccei Gemini praefecti alae Lucio Lolliano Avito Tito Statilio Maximo consulibus X Kalendas Apriles in schola Pollia quod verba facta sunt de honoranda morte Clodiae Anthianillae quid de ea re fieri placeret de ea re ita censuerunt cum Clodia Anthianilla splendidissima puella et cuius incrementa etiam supra aetatem florentia inter ornamenta municipi nostri sperabantur acervissima morte rapta sit parentibus suis Clodio Pollioni patrono municipi nostri splendidissimo equiti Romano et bene de re publica nostra merito et Seiae Quintilliae matri ornatae feminae quorum dolori publica municipi nostri tristitia consentit placere decuriones et in illorum solacium et in memoriam honestissimae puellae locum posteritatis dari item statuam quam frequentissimo loco publice poni censuit Lucius Clodius Luci filius Pollio pater piissimae filiae honore accepto impensam remisit\n
EDCS-16400385.translation: To Clodia, daughter of Lucius, Anthianilla. In the consulship of Marcus Cocceius Geminius, Prefect of the cavalry wing, Lucius Lollianus Avitus, and Titus Statilius Maximus, on the tenth day before the Kalends of April. In the Pollia school, where a discussion was held concerning the honorable death of Clodia Anthianilla and what decision should be made regarding her. The following was decreed: Since Clodia Anthianilla, a most splendid girl, whose flourishing development was expected to surpass her age among the ornaments of our municipality, was snatched away by a most bitter death, leaving her parents, Clodius Pollio, patron of our municipality, a most splendid Roman knight, and one who has well deserved of our state, and Seia Quintilla, her honored mother, whose grief is shared by the public sorrow of our municipality, the decurions have decreed that a site be given for posterity, both for their consolation and in memory of the most honorable girl. They also decreed that a statue be publicly erected in the most frequented place. Lucius Clodius Pollio, son of Lucius, father of his most devoted daughter, having received this honor, waived the expense.
EDCS-16400385.summary: This decree from a municipality honors the memory of a young girl, Clodia Anthianilla, who died prematurely, by dedicating public space and a statue, funded by her grieving father, who then renounced the expense.

EDCS-16900090.text_edition: \n Lucius Boionius Zeno veteranus de Phryge triere nauta vixit annos XCV hic situs\n
EDCS-16900090.translation: Lucius Boionius Zeno, veteran, from Phrygia, rower, sailor, lived 95 years. Buried here.
EDCS-16900090.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a veteran sailor named Lucius Boionius Zeno, indicating his origin and lifespan.

EDCS-16900182.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Rutili Macedonis curatoris arkae sic Puteolanorum aedilis IIviri edente Beneventi exornato munere diebus IIII feris numero IIII ursis XVI noxeis sic IIII et ceteris herbariis Licinia Marcella marito karissimo ex quo et liberos IIII enixa est cum quo vixit annis XVIII ob obsequium omnem erga ipsam qua acvixit sic bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-16900182.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Marcus Rutilius Macedo, curator of the public treasury of the Puteolans, aedile, duumvir. Presented by Beneventus, embellished by the munificence of the games on four days: four wild beasts, sixteen bears, four noxious animals, and other wild animals. Licinia Marcella made this for her dearest husband, with whom she lived for 18 years, and by whom she bore four children, for his deserving conduct towards her during their entire life.
EDCS-16900182.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a wife to her husband, Marcus Rutilius Macedo, a former magistrate, commemorating his public service and their marital life, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17100001.text_edition: \n Pentascinensibus thermis quae longo temporis tractu intercepto aquae meatu lavacris frequentari desierant undis largioribus afluentibus nymphalem aquam in meliores usus sua liberalitate Caius Furius Cai libertus Togius Quintilius induxit lio Petrio viro perfectissimo\n
EDCS-17100001.translation: To the Pentascinian baths, which, after a long period of time, had ceased to be frequented due to the interruption of the water flow. With more abundant waters flowing, Gaius Furius Togius Quintilius, freedman of Gaius, son of Titus, introduced the nymphal water for better use through his generosity, to the most perfect man, Lucius Petrius.
EDCS-17100001.summary: This inscription records the restoration of water flow to public baths by a generous individual, Gaius Furius Togius Quintilius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17100161.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo conservatori domus Augustae Marcus Cocceius Augusti libertus Pudens ob honorem prohedriae locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-17100161.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, protector of the Augustan house. Marcus Cocceius Pudens, freedman of Augustus, was granted the place of the prohedria (front seat) by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-17100161.summary: This inscription records the granting of a distinguished seating privilege (prohedria) to Marcus Cocceius Pudens, a freedman of the emperor, by the local council, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500401.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely an ownership mark or dedication, mentioning Marcus Annius Verus.

EDCS-17500404.translation: Of Gaius Betitius Pius and Fuscinilla.
EDCS-17500404.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely an ownership mark or dedication, mentioning Gaius Betitius Pius and Fuscinilla.

EDCS-17500408.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely an ownership mark or dedication, mentioning Gaius Dossennius Martialis.

EDCS-17500412.translation: Belonging to Fortis, son of Cornelius Cethegus.
EDCS-17500412.summary: This is a private funerary inscription, likely a tombstone or epitaph, for Fortis, son of Cornelius Cethegus.

EDCS-17500568.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, rebuilt the Appian Way for 17,200 paces, which had been lost due to age, with an additional 1,147,000 sesterces, with landowners contributing 569,100 sesterces.
EDCS-17500568.summary: This is a public works inscription from the reign of Hadrian, detailing the emperor's reconstruction of the Appian Way and the costs involved.

EDCS-17600088.translation: For our lords Diocletian and Maximian Augusti and the Caesars Constantius and Maximian. 3 miles.
EDCS-17600088.summary: This is a milestone inscription, possibly marking a road, dedicated to the emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Maximian.

EDCS-17600112.translation: To the pious Successa, wife of Betitius.
EDCS-17600112.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely an epitaph, dedicated to Successa, wife of Betitius.

EDCS-17600114.summary: This is a private inscription, likely indicating ownership or a dedication, for Gaius Popillius Priscus.

EDCS-17600117.translation: From the freedmen and actresses of Salutaris, husband of Marianna Valeriana, a most distinguished woman.
EDCS-17600117.summary: This is a funerary or dedication inscription set up by freedmen and actresses for their patrons, Salutaris and Marianna Valeriana.

EDCS-17600328.translation: 62. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, built the road from Benevento to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600328.summary: This is a road construction inscription from the reign of Trajan, detailing his personal funding for the road from Benevento to Brundisium.

EDCS-17600329.translation: 63. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, built the road from Benevento to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600329.summary: This is a road construction inscription from the reign of Trajan, detailing his personal funding for the road from Benevento to Brundisium.

EDCS-17600334.translation: To our lord Iovius Licinius, the always victorious Augustus.
EDCS-17600334.summary: This is a dedication, possibly to an emperor or a divinity, for Iovius Licinius Augustus.

EDCS-17600336.translation: To Emperor Caesar [Flavius Valerius] Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, son of the deified Constantius, consul for the 3rd time, imperator for the 8th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-17600336.summary: This is a dedication inscription honoring Constantine the Great, listing his titles and offices.

EDCS-17600337.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of his country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600337.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding for the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-17600338.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of his country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600338.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding for the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-17600342.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of his country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600342.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding for the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-17600343.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of his country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600343.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding for the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-17600344.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of his country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600344.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding for the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-17600345.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of his country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600345.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding for the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-17600346.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of his country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600346.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding for the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-17600349.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of his country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600349.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding for the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-17600350.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Constantine, Flavius Valerius Constantinus, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, consul for the 3rd time, imperator for the 9th time, proconsul, liberator of the world of lands.
EDCS-17600350.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great, honoring his titles and role as 'liberator of the world', likely from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-17600352.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of his country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600352.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding for the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely dating to his reign (98-117 AD).

EDCS-17600354.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600354.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding of the road from Benevento to Brundisium, dated to his 13th tribunician power year (108/109 AD).

EDCS-17600355.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600355.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding of the road from Benevento to Brundisium, dated to his 13th tribunician power year (108/109 AD).

EDCS-17600359.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17600359.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding of the road from Benevento to Brundisium, dated to his 13th tribunician power year (108/109 AD).

EDCS-17600369.translation: To Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, consul for the 3rd time, imperator for the 9th time, father of the country, proconsul.
EDCS-17600369.summary: This is a dedication honoring Emperor Constantine I, likely on a public monument, listing his titles and consulship.

EDCS-17600370.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, proconsul, restored the road of Hercules to its former appearance.
EDCS-17600370.summary: This inscription records Emperor Maxentius's restoration of the Via Herculea, dated to his second consulship (308 or 311 AD).

EDCS-17700710.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a dedication or ownership marker, for Marcus Antonius Rufus.

EDCS-17700794.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the deified [Julius Caesar], consul for the 11th time, holder of tribunician power for the 7th time, by decree of the Senate.
EDCS-17700794.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Augustus, likely on a monument funded or approved by the Senate, dated to his 11th consulship and 7th tribunician power year (late 20s BCE).

EDCS-17700798.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, son of the deified Constantius, born for the good of the state, 14 miles.
EDCS-17700798.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine I, son of Constantius I, possibly marking a distance of 14 miles for a road project.

EDCS-17700807.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17700807.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding of the road from Benevento to Brundisium, dated to his 13th tribunician power year (108/109 AD).

EDCS-17700809.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17700809.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's personal funding of the road from Benevento to Brundisium, dated to his 13th tribunician power year (108/109 AD).

EDCS-17700812.translation: To Licinianus Licinius, the German, Sarmatian, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Emperor, 15 miles.
EDCS-17700812.summary: This is an milestone inscription recording a distance of 15 miles dedicated to Emperor Licinianus Licinius.

EDCS-17700815.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Constantius, Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Emperor, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, Liberator of the Roman State.
EDCS-17700815.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription for Emperor Constantine the Great, listing his titles and acknowledging his role in restoring the Roman state.

EDCS-17800041.text_edition: \n LXXXII Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae viam a Benevento Brundisium pecunia sua fecit\n
EDCS-17800041.translation: 82. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17800041.summary: This is a milestone inscription stating that Emperor Trajan funded and built the road from Benevento to Brundisium.

EDCS-17800042.translation: 82. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17800042.summary: This is a milestone inscription stating that Emperor Trajan funded and built the road from Benevento to Brundisium.

EDCS-17800043.translation: 99. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17800043.summary: This is a milestone inscription stating that Emperor Trajan funded and built the road from Benevento to Brundisium.

EDCS-17800044.translation: 96. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17800044.summary: This is a milestone inscription stating that Emperor Trajan funded and built the road from Benevento to Brundisium.

EDCS-17800046.translation: 104. Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, made the road from Benevento to Brundisium at his own expense. To our Lord Julian, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered and Triumphant, always Augustus, good for the state, born for the state. To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrianus Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, descendant of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus.
EDCS-17800046.summary: This inscription combines a milestone of Emperor Trajan with a lengthy dedication to Emperor Julian and an incomplete dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus.

EDCS-18000271.translation: Marcus Caesellius, son of Marcus, [---], IIvir with aedilician power.
EDCS-18000271.summary: This inscription records Marcus Caesellius, who held the office of IIvir with aedilician power.

EDCS-18000272.translation: Marcia Quartilla, daughter of Lucius, lived 35 years. She is buried here; the funeral and burial were provided by the public.
EDCS-18000272.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcia Quartilla, who died at age 35 and whose burial was publicly funded.

EDCS-18000274.translation: Lucius Pacilius Taurus, priest of the Mother Goddess, and of the goddess Syria, and of the rites of Isis, lived 65 years. And Lucius Publilius Auctus, his father, lived 70 years. And Publilia Nice, freedwoman of Lucius, his mother, lived 100 years. They are buried here.
EDCS-18000274.summary: This is a family tomb inscription recording the ages and priesthoods of Lucius Pacilius Taurus and his parents.

EDCS-18000275.translation: Philon, son of Antas, grandson of Antipater, from Tyre, lived 60 years. Here lies Marcia Syntyche, freedwoman of Gaius.
EDCS-18000275.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Philon and Marcia Syntyche, likely from the Roman Imperial period, recording their names and lifespan.

EDCS-18000285.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Valerius Sabinus has paid his vow.
EDCS-18000285.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Valerius Sabinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18000286.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Septima, slave of Passenius Rufus, lived 4 years. Here lies her parents for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-18000286.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Septima, slave of Passenius Rufus, who died at the age of 4, and was memorialized by her parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18000287.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Prosdexis, recording her name and lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200058.translation: Agasia, daughter of Decimus, of the Caesellii family, lies here.
EDCS-18200058.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Agasia, daughter of Decimus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200059.translation: By the will of Mani Albinius Philotimus, freedman of Mani, under the supervision of Mani Albinius Philotimus, freedman of Mani, and Mani Albinius Sampsaeus, freedman of Mani.
EDCS-18200059.summary: This is a funerary inscription detailing the execution of a will for the burial of Mani Albinius Philotimus and Mani Albinius Sampsaeus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200060.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Ammia Strunia, his most devoted mother, lived 70 years. Here lies.
EDCS-18200060.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Ammia Strunia, mother of the deceased, who lived 70 years, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200061.translation: Marcus Ant[---] lived 90 years. Here lies. Iucud[---]
EDCS-18200061.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ant[---], recording his lifespan and burial place, with a partial name for another individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200063.translation: Gaius Antonius Severus, son of Gaius, lived 55 years. Here lies. [---]sae lived 9 months. Here lies.
EDCS-18200063.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Antonius Severus, recording his lifespan and burial, and also mentioning another individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200064.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Antonia Salvia, recording her burial place, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200065.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Caecilius Capito, likely on a monument or tomb, from an unspecified date.

EDCS-18200066.translation: Caesia Callityche; Gaius Caesius Epicadus rests here.
EDCS-18200066.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Caesia Callityche and Gaius Caesius Epicadus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200067.translation: Anteros, freedman of Publius Calavius Zethus and Amphionis (sons of Decimus), rests here. Farewell, traveler.
EDCS-18200067.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Anteros, a freedman of Publius Calavius Zethus and Amphionis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200068.translation: Publius Calavius Nicephorus, freedman of Publius, [in a plot] 20 feet wide in the field, [and] 20 feet in frontage.
EDCS-18200068.summary: This inscription appears to record land dimensions, specifically 20 feet of frontage and depth, for Publius Calavius Nicephorus, a freedman, likely related to property ownership in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200069.text_edition: \n C(aius) Calidius Cai filius Palatina Optatus praefectus cohortis II Thessalorum \n
EDCS-18200069.translation: Gaius Calidius Optatus, son of Caius, of the Palatina tribe, prefect of the First Cohort of Thessalonians.
EDCS-18200069.summary: This is a cursus honorum inscription for Gaius Calidius Optatus, who served as prefect of a cohort, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200071.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription for Restitutus, a freedman of Servius Orfitus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200072.translation: The Curia Helpis lived for 13 years.
EDCS-18200072.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Curia Helpis, who died at 13 years of age, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200073.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that Cyclas is buried here, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200074.summary: This tombstone records the death of Epigone at the age of 10, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200075.translation: Publius Ficellius Vitlus lived for 35 years; he rests here.
EDCS-18200075.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan and burial of Publius Ficellius Vitlus, who died at 35 years old, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200076.translation: To the Divine Shades, Flavia Zosime.
EDCS-18200076.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Flavia Zosime, likely a tombstone or epitaph.

EDCS-18200077.text_edition: \n Aulus Gabinius Thychicus sic vixit annos XXIX hic situs est\n
EDCS-18200077.translation: Aulus Gabinius Thychicus lived 29 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-18200077.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan and burial of Aulus Gabinius Thychicus.

EDCS-18200078.translation: Publius Gerellanus Acindynus lived 50 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-18200078.summary: This inscription marks the burial of Publius Gerellanus Acindynus, stating his age at death.

EDCS-18200079.translation: She lived 6 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-18200079.summary: This epitaph records the short life of an unnamed female child, stating she is buried here.

EDCS-18200080.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the name and age of a child, Lascivia Calidia, who lived 4 years.

EDCS-18200081.translation: Gaius Marcius Carpus, freedman of Gaius, lived 68 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-18200081.summary: This epitaph details the life and burial of Gaius Marcius Carpus, a freedman, who died at age 68.

EDCS-18200082.translation: Lucius Munatius Salvius, father of these, lived 37 years; Gaius Lanius Lalus lived 30 years.
EDCS-18200082.summary: This inscription appears to be a combined funerary monument commemorating Lucius Munatius Salvius and Gaius Lanius Lalus, possibly father and son or relatives.

EDCS-18200083.translation: Lucius Negilius Esychus, freedman of Lucius, lived 80 years.
EDCS-18200083.summary: This epitaph records the name and age of Lucius Negilius Esychus, a freedman who lived to be 80.

EDCS-18200085.summary: This is a simple funerary dedication to Octavia, a freedwoman.

EDCS-18200086.translation: Pacilia Chrysarium, freedwoman of Publius, is buried here.
EDCS-18200086.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Pacilia Chrysarium, a freedwoman.

EDCS-18200087.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Decimus Pactumeius Onesimus vixit annos XXII bene merenti\n
EDCS-18200087.translation: To the divine shades. Decimus Pactumeius Onesimus lived 22 years. To one deserving.
EDCS-18200087.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Decimus Pactumeius Onesimus, who lived 22 years.

EDCS-18200088.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Caius Peti[---] and Ianua[---].

EDCS-18200090.translation: [---]V. [---]ria. [---] Prima. In the front, 20 feet; in the ground, 30 feet.
EDCS-18200090.summary: This inscription seems to record land measurements, possibly for a tomb or property boundary.

EDCS-18200091.translation: Sileia Amaryllis, freedwoman of Publius, lived 36 years. Here lies buried.
EDCS-18200091.summary: A funerary inscription for Sileia Amaryllis, a freedwoman, who lived 36 years.

EDCS-18200092.translation: Tituria Zosima lived 45 years. Here lies buried.
EDCS-18200092.summary: A funerary inscription for Tituria Zosima, who lived 45 years.

EDCS-18200094.translation: Marcus Veratius Probatus, freedman of the woman, lived 20 years, and Sperata. Mother. They lie buried here.
EDCS-18200094.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Veratius Probatus and Sperata, stating their ages and that they are buried here.

EDCS-18200095.translation: [---]. Vitalis lived 42 years. He lies buried here.
EDCS-18200095.summary: A funerary inscription for Vitalis, who lived 42 years.

EDCS-18200105.translation: Pretiosus, bishop of the holy, catholic church of Brundisium, was laid to rest in the sleep of peace on the sixth day, which is the 15th day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-18200105.summary: An epitaph for Pretiosus, a bishop of the church in Brundisium, who died on a specific date.

EDCS-18200107.translation: [---] Ianuaria lived 40 years. She lies buried here. [---] Linatius [---] Zosimanus.
EDCS-18200107.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for Ianuaria, who lived 40 years, and possibly mentions Linatius Zosimanus.

EDCS-18200108.translation: To the divine shades. Pompeius Pannychus lies buried here. Lucius Cassius Fortunatus. N C A.
EDCS-18200108.summary: A funerary inscription for Pompeius Pannychus, with an unknown abbreviation 'N C A' possibly added by Lucius Cassius Fortunatus.

EDCS-18200109.translation: Zosime lived for 12 years; she is buried here with her mother.
EDCS-18200109.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young girl named Zosime, noting her age and burial with her mother.

EDCS-18200117.translation: Gaius Melsonius Hercolis, freedman of Gnaeus, a gift.
EDCS-18200117.summary: An inscription recording a dedication or offering by Gaius Melsonius Hercolis, a freedman, possibly to the god Hercules.

EDCS-18200120.translation: Lucius Allius Stobera, son of Lucius, of the Scaptia tribe, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica, lived for 60 years and served for 25 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-18200120.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Allius Stobera, a veteran of Legio V Macedonica, detailing his age at death and years of service.

EDCS-18200121.translation: Lucius Pompeius, son of Lucius, veteran of the Legio XII [?], is buried here.
EDCS-18200121.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Pompeius, a veteran of Legio XII, with incomplete information about his legion.

EDCS-18200122.translation: Publius Puticius Niger, son of Publius, of the Maecia tribe, a soldier of Legio VI, discharged, is buried here.
EDCS-18200122.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Puticius Niger, a discharged soldier of Legio VI, noting his tribal affiliation.

EDCS-18200123.summary: A fragmentary epitaph for Camax, an Adramytenian, possibly indicating age and origin.

EDCS-18200124.translation: Lucius [?] Capha[?] lived for 35 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-18200124.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Capha[?], noting his age at death.

EDCS-18200125.translation: Gaius Atilius Pudens lived for 55 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-18200125.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Atilius Pudens, noting his age at death.

EDCS-18200127.translation: Dafne lived for 30 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-18200127.summary: A funerary epitaph for Dafne, noting her age at death.

EDCS-18200128.translation: Gaius Domitius Diomedes is buried here.
EDCS-18200128.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Domitius Diomedes.

EDCS-18200129.translation: Domitia Aina lived 60 years. She is buried here. For Felicio, who deserved well.
EDCS-18200129.summary: This is a tombstone for Domitia Aina, who lived 60 years and is buried here, with a dedication to Felicio.

EDCS-18200130.translation: Thyillus, slave of Nepos, made this for his beloved Euaristus, who lived 5 years and 11 months.
EDCS-18200130.summary: A funerary epitaph for Euaristus, slave of Nepos, erected by Thyillus, who died at the age of 5 years and 11 months.

EDCS-18200131.summary: An incomplete inscription beginning with the name Publius Gere-.
