EDCS-08201590.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription identifying the deceased as Publius Stenius Salvius, freedman of Publius.

EDCS-08201599.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a tombstone, naming Vesonia Calixsta.

EDCS-08201607.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that seems to record the name of a person, Marcus Mam[---].

EDCS-08201618.translation: Sextus, freedman of the woman Pai, lived.
EDCS-08201618.summary: This fragmentary inscription records Sextus, identified as a freedman of a woman named Pai.

EDCS-08201624.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that seems to be a dedication or a funerary text involving a freedman and his patron.

EDCS-08201634.translation: Timotea, daughter of Sigif, who lived for four years.
EDCS-08201634.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Timotea, daughter of Sigif, at the age of four.

EDCS-08201635.translation: Calpurnia Arche, freedwoman of Lucius, for herself and for Lucius Calpurnius her patron, the Augustalis, gratefully, and for Euantus her father, and for Calpurnia Nardi, her grandmother, and for Lucius freedman of Calpurnius Primigenius, and for Calpurnius [---].
EDCS-08201635.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Calpurnia Arche, freedwoman of Lucius, dedicating the burial to herself, her patron, father, grandmother, and other relatives, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08201636.translation: Lucius Plotius Seleucus, freedman of Vicinus, is buried here.
EDCS-08201636.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Lucius Plotius Seleucus, a freedman of Vicinus.

EDCS-08201637.translation: Publius Luc Decimus, Lucius Trebius, Lucius Vecillius, the board of four men serving as quinquennales.
EDCS-08201637.summary: This inscription records four men who served together as quinquennales (officials holding office for five years), likely dating to the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-08201638.translation: Marcus Villius Speratus, son of Marcus, of the Velina tribe, was granted this place on account of the quinquennial magistracy, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08201638.summary: This inscription records the granting of a public space to Marcus Villius Speratus for his quinquennial office, by local councillors.

EDCS-08201640.translation: To Gaius Catilius Sabinus, from the guild of woodworkers.
EDCS-08201640.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Gaius Catilius Sabinus by the dendrophoroi, likely a guild or collegium.

EDCS-08201641.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ammia Sabina, mother, made this for her well-deserving daughter, Ammia Primigenia, who lived 25 years, 10 months.
EDCS-08201641.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ammia Primigenia, set up by her mother.

EDCS-08201643.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Baebius Aper made this for his most dutiful father, Gaius Baebius Autillus, who deserved well.
EDCS-08201643.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Baebius Autillus, set up by his son Gaius Baebius Aper.

EDCS-08201667.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Haerenus Modenus, son of Vibbia NE M LLI.
EDCS-08201667.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph for Gaius Haerenus Modenus.

EDCS-08201669.translation: Quintus Vellaeus Successus, freedman of Quintus, an Augustalis, paved the street for 880 feet.
EDCS-08201669.summary: This inscription records a public works project, the paving of a street, funded by Quintus Vellaeus Successus.

EDCS-08201670.translation: Sacred to Venus. Primus, the treasurer.
EDCS-08201670.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the goddess Venus by a person named Primus, identified as an arcarius (treasurer).

EDCS-08201671.translation: By public decree of the decurions, to the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul for the second time.
EDCS-08201671.summary: This inscription records a public dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius, detailing his imperial titles and the authority behind the dedication.

EDCS-08201672.translation: To Aelius Antoninus, son of Caesar, Caesar D T, Emperor, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-08201672.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Aelius Antoninus, likely referring to a member of the imperial family, possibly Commodus.

EDCS-08201674.translation: Polytimus, freedman of Marcus, son of Galeria Primo, who served as a decemvir for judicial cases, tribune of the VII Gemina Felix legion in Hispania Citerior, quaestor, curule aedile, curator of the Aurelian Way, proconsul of Lycia and Pamphylia, and consul.
EDCS-08201674.summary: This inscription details the extensive career of a freedman named Polytimus, who served in various high-ranking administrative and military positions, culminating in the consulship.

EDCS-08201675.translation: To Lucius Antistius Paulus, son of Quintus, of the Galeria tribe, a four-man official of the quinquennial period and patron of the municipium, the urban populace, by a collected levy, has granted this place by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-08201675.summary: This inscription records the granting of a place to a local patron by the urban populace, as ordered by the decurions, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201679.translation: Lucius Mindius Maximus, son of Marcius, of the Galeria tribe.
EDCS-08201679.summary: This inscription is a simple funerary dedication identifying an individual, Lucius Mindius Maximus, by name, tribe, and father's name, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201680.translation: To Pontius Marcellinus, of the Galeria tribe, a four-man official judging justly, and to Oppia Januaria, parents.
EDCS-08201680.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Pontius Marcellinus and Oppia Januaria, who were parents and a four-man official, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201681.translation: Gaius Umbrius Eudrastus Fortunatus, a four-man official, with his own funds, [erected] the measurements according to the law of the state.
EDCS-08201681.summary: This inscription records the erection of public measurements by Gaius Umbrius Eudrastus Fortunatus, a four-man official, at his own expense, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201683.translation: Apriseius took care of its erection, on the occasion of whose dedication [he gave] to each of the decurions 5 denarii and to the people residing within the walls 1 denarius each.
EDCS-08201683.summary: This inscription details the distribution of money to decurions and citizens upon the dedication of a monument erected by Apriseius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201685.translation: Into which sarcophagus I have brought [my body], into which, once my body has been received, it shall never be permissible for anyone to approach or disturb my bones, neither son, nor grandson, nor anyone related by affinity; but if anyone dares to break or open [it], he shall pay to the public treasury a penalty of one thousand folles. However, Nevia Prisca, if she remains alive until the day of her death, shall be received there beside her husband.
EDCS-08201685.summary: This is a detailed funerary inscription setting strict conditions for the use of a sarcophagus and stipulating penalties for violations, while making a specific exception for the deceased's wife, Nevia Prisca, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201686.translation: Vebia Maria made [this] for Publius Accius Marcianus, her well-deserving husband, with whom she lived for 12 years.
EDCS-08201686.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commemorating Vebia Maria's husband, Publius Accius Marcianus, and stating the duration of their marriage, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201687.translation: Lucius Albius, son of Caius, of the Poblilia tribe.
EDCS-08201687.summary: This inscription is a simple funerary dedication identifying an individual, Lucius Albius, by name, tribe, and father's name, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201688.translation: To the Divine Shades. Secundianus made [this] for his well-deserving son, Apollonius.
EDCS-08201688.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Secundianus to his son Apollonius, invoking the Divine Shades, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201689.summary: This inscription is a simple funerary dedication identifying an individual, Numerius Lepidius, by name and father's name, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08201691.translation: To his dearest daughter, Oppius Honos, father, and Flavia Romania, mother, parents [dedicated this].
EDCS-08201691.summary: A dedication from parents to their dearly loved daughter.

EDCS-08201692.translation: Mirtilus, father, made this for his deserving daughter Prima.
EDCS-08201692.summary: A dedication from a father to his deserving daughter.

EDCS-08201693.translation: To the divine spirits: Ursulus made this for his deserving father Secundianus.
EDCS-08201693.summary: A dedication from a son to his deserving father.

EDCS-08300227.translation: The field, house, garden, and tomb of Cleon, physician; Cai Marci, freedman of Caius, his freedwoman Haline, wife, lives. And all the rest.
EDCS-08300227.summary: A funerary inscription describing the property of a physician and his freedwoman wife, likely from the Roman period in Greece.

EDCS-08400169.translation: Bictorius and Iusta have fulfilled their promises to the Church of Lucera.
EDCS-08400169.summary: A record of a fulfillment of vows to the Church of Lucera by Bictorius and Iusta.

EDCS-08400170.translation: Maxima fulfilled her vow to the Church of Lucera.
EDCS-08400170.summary: A record of a vow fulfilled to the Church of Lucera by Maxima.

EDCS-08500203.translation: To the divine spirits: Aulus Postymius Seneca, son of Aulus, lived for 4 years.
EDCS-08500203.summary: A funerary inscription recording the short life of a child.

EDCS-08500205.summary: An inscription identifying Zoves as a tribune of the plebs, likely a political or official record.

EDCS-08500220.translation: Sacred to Luna: Aulus Terentius [dedicated this].
EDCS-08500220.summary: A dedication to the goddess Luna by Aulus Terentius.

EDCS-08500222.translation: Ufia, daughter of Meris, four months and nine days old, is buried here.
EDCS-08500222.summary: A funerary inscription for a very young child, detailing her parentage and age.

EDCS-08900218.summary: This inscription is a simple name, likely marking a tomb or property belonging to Marcus Negilius Secundus.

EDCS-08900248.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Felicula, wife of Caius Visius, made this; she lived 2 years, 3 months. Here she lies.
EDCS-08900248.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Felicula, wife of Caius Visius, and states her age at death.

EDCS-08900888.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Lartidius Bus, a shipowner, dedicated this, [having vowed it] for himself with a prosperous voyage.
EDCS-08900888.summary: This votive inscription is dedicated to Jupiter by Marcus Lartidius Bus, a shipowner, possibly to thank the god for a safe journey.

EDCS-08900889.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucius Valerius Sabinus gladly paid his vow with the full complement.
EDCS-08900889.summary: This votive inscription records Lucius Valerius Sabinus fulfilling a vow made to Jupiter.

EDCS-08900891.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Cordius Aquillinus paid his vow gladly with the full complement of the Rhedones and Meduari.
EDCS-08900891.summary: This votive inscription indicates that Gaius Cordius Aquillinus fulfilled a vow made to Jupiter, possibly in conjunction with two communities named Rhedones and Meduari.

EDCS-08900893.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Geminia Felicula lived 65 years. Here she lies. [This was set up by] Gaius Marcius.
EDCS-08900893.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Geminia Felicula, stating her age at death and identifying Gaius Marcius as the person who set up the monument.

EDCS-09001234.translation: To Silvanus Curtianus, Lucius Staius Herodotus gladly paid his vow. Νεικετι [---].
EDCS-09001234.summary: This votive inscription records Lucius Staius Herodotus fulfilling a vow to Silvanus Curtianus.

EDCS-09001236.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lupu rests here in the sleep of peace.
EDCS-09001236.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of a person named Lupu and their resting place.

EDCS-09001237.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Winelaupo rests here in the sleep of peace.
EDCS-09001237.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of a person named Winelaupo and their resting place.

EDCS-09001241.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Quinta Synphorus, a fellow slave deserving of kindness, and to Elaphe, mother; she lived 22 years.
EDCS-09001241.summary: This funerary epitaph honors a deserving fellow slave named Quinta Synphorus and her mother Elaphe, and records the age of death for Elaphe.

EDCS-09001243.translation: In the fourteenth indiction, here lies Ilarianus, who had not yet completed nine months, whom the tomb received dead, and whose soul will only see the expanses of the kingdom; snatched away barely eight months old, the infant causes immense grief to his parents by his cruel death.
EDCS-09001243.summary: This is a Latin epitaph for Ilarianus, a child who died before the age of one year, likely from the 14th indiction.

EDCS-09001252.translation: Here rests Leontia of holy memory, who lived more or less 55 years; she was buried on the 5th day before the Kalends of February, in the consulship of Flavius Volusianus, most illustrious.
EDCS-09001252.summary: This is a Christian epitaph for Leontia, detailing her lifespan and burial date, referencing the consulship of Flavius Volusianus.

EDCS-09001253.summary: A very brief epitaph simply stating the name of the deceased, Minius.

EDCS-09200130.translation: V. Camuria Nice from her own property for Felicioni.
EDCS-09200130.summary: This dedication records that V. Camuria Nice set up something for Felicioni at her own expense.

EDCS-09200182.translation: Sextus Aemilius Primigenius lived 50 years, 3 months, 9 days; buried.
EDCS-09200182.summary: An epitaph stating the name, age at death, and burial status of Sextus Aemilius Primigenius.

EDCS-09300168.translation: Maria Optata Tertia, freedwoman of Lucius, erected this for herself and her mother Iucunda, who is buried.
EDCS-09300168.summary: An epitaph for Maria Optata Tertia, erected by herself for herself and her mother, Iucunda, who was a freedwoman.

EDCS-09300174.translation: Optatus, slave of Sextus Lucius, buried. His mother Quinta and his father Cerdo set this up.
EDCS-09300174.summary: An epitaph for Optatus, a slave, erected by his parents Quinta and Cerdo.

EDCS-09300175.translation: This slave of Thuridae lived 30 years. Thuridia Anthusa, his unhappy wife, erected this for herself and her husband.
EDCS-09300175.summary: An epitaph for an unnamed slave of Thuridae, erected by his wife Thuridia Anthusa, detailing his age at death.

EDCS-09300180.translation: Cypare, consort of Lucius Sestius Primus and his daughter Logas, erected this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-09300180.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Cypare for her husband, Lucius Sestius Primus, and also for their daughter Logas.

EDCS-09300183.summary: A simple inscription indicating ownership or belonging, likely for Lucius Vinius Myrtilus.

EDCS-09300189.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Threptus, slave of Lae, lived 50 years. Nice placed this for her well-deserving husband and for herself.
EDCS-09300189.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a wife for her deceased husband, Threptus, and herself.

EDCS-09300878.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Marcia Ianuaria.

EDCS-09400182.translation: For Lucius Cornelius Sulla Felix, son of Lucius, dictator, patron.
EDCS-09400182.summary: This is an inscription honoring Lucius Cornelius Sulla Felix, a dictator and patron.

EDCS-09401401.translation: Here rests Augusta, wife of Bonus, a man of praiseworthy character, daughter of Isatis, granddaughter of Symonatis, from the Lypiensian people, aged 20 years and 3 months, on the 8th day before the Kalends of October, in the 15th indiction, during the consulship of Valerius. Augusta, Amen, peace upon her remnant.
EDCS-09401401.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Augusta, detailing her family lineage, age, and date of death.

EDCS-09401403.translation: Decumanus, Marcus Fulvius, son of Marcus, Caius Sempronius, son of Tiberius, triumvirs for judging and assigning lands.
EDCS-09401403.summary: This inscription records the names and official titles of three men serving as triumvirs for land distribution.

EDCS-09401404.translation: Caius Calvius Philomustus is buried here.
EDCS-09401404.summary: This is a simple tombstone indicating the burial place of Caius Calvius Philomustus.

EDCS-09700109.translation: The Gentiles of Magius made [this] for the holy Deivetus.
EDCS-09700109.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the 'Gentiles' of Magius to a deity named Deivetus.

EDCS-09700114.translation: The splendid order of Capua, always obedient, also with the subsequent deference of his descendants, dedicated this statue to their original patron and restorer, Pontius Proserius Paulinus, a most distinguished man and consular governor of Campania.
EDCS-09700114.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue by the city of Capua to their esteemed former governor and patron, Pontius Proserius Paulinus.

EDCS-09700897.summary: This is a tombstone recording the short lifespan of Nymphius.

EDCS-09700925.translation: Lucius Curius, son of Lucius, of the First Legion.
EDCS-09700925.summary: This inscription likely marks the grave or commemorates Lucius Curius, a soldier serving in the First Legion.

EDCS-09700928.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus Ulpius Moderatus erected this for his father, Gnaeus Rustius Moderatus, a well-deserving man.
EDCS-09700928.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Ulpius Moderatus to his father, Gnaeus Rustius Moderatus.

EDCS-09701409.translation: The city council of Benevento erects this statue to Turranius Decentius Benignus, a most distinguished man, count of the first rank, acting prefect of the praetorian guard, their patron, in recognition of his outstanding benefactions, even now as a private citizen.
EDCS-09701409.summary: A statue erected by the city council of Benevento to Turranius Decentius Benignus, a distinguished official and patron, recognizing his benefactions.

EDCS-09701410.translation: The entire college of the Leontiani, both the juniors and those whose fathers, grandfathers, and great-grandfathers were members, have erected this statue as a perpetual gift of love to Pullius Argolicus, a most perfect man, their patron, so that it may renew the old services he rendered to his family, in his presence.
EDCS-09701410.summary: A statue erected by the Leontiani college to their patron, Pullius Argolicus, to honor his family's long-standing service and his own contributions.

EDCS-10701363.translation: To a well-deserving mother and loving parent. Cornelius Niceta [erected this].
EDCS-10701363.summary: A funerary dedication by Cornelius Niceta to his mother.

EDCS-10701364.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Crispieas Ampliataes lived 18 years, 2 months. Carita and Phoebe [erected this] to their well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-10701364.summary: A funerary inscription for Crispieas Ampliataes, who died at the age of 18, dedicated by her daughters.

EDCS-10900571.translation: The burial of the good memory of Timotheus, on the Kalends of December, in the consulship of our Lord Anastasius Augustus and the most distinguished man Rufus.
EDCS-10900571.summary: A burial record for Timotheus, dated by the consulship of Anastasius Augustus and Rufus.

EDCS-10900572.translation: The burial of Cerviolus, my dearest companion, six days before the Kalends of June, in the consulship of Maburtus, who lived more or less 41 years.
EDCS-10900572.summary: A funerary inscription for Cerviolus, a companion who lived approximately 41 years, dated by the consulship of Maburtus.

EDCS-10900576.translation: Here rests CE Anastasi o who lived
EDCS-10900576.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription indicating the resting place of Anastasi o.

EDCS-10900577.translation: Caelestine, may you live in God and accomplish more, dedicate better, happily.
EDCS-10900577.summary: A hopeful epitaph for Caelestine, wishing her a blessed afterlife.

EDCS-11600028.summary: An ownership mark or dedication related to Tiberius Claudius Fortunatus.

EDCS-11800661.translation: By the authority of Sextilia Dionysia.
EDCS-11800661.summary: This inscription records a dedication made under the authority of Sextilia Dionysia.

EDCS-12400491.translation: Restitutus lives for himself and his wife, and his sons, and they live for their sons; what the sons ought to have done for their father, the father has done for his sons.
EDCS-12400491.summary: This inscription is a funerary epitaph where the deceased records his own provision for his family, reflecting on filial duty.

EDCS-12400492.translation: Seppius Ptunus, from Mnia, paid for this with his own money.
EDCS-12400492.summary: This inscription records that Seppius Ptunus paid for a construction or monument with his own funds.

EDCS-12400493.translation: Gaius Pompullius, son of Publius, of the Horatia tribe, chief centurion, military tribune, prefect of cavalry, and his wife Gemella, daughter of Publius of Careia, by the arrangement of his wife Gemella and their freedman Philodamus.
EDCS-12400493.summary: This inscription records the careers and family of Gaius Pompullius, a military officer, and his wife Gemella, likely on a monument they commissioned.

EDCS-12400495.translation: Quintus Caesutius Trypo, freedman of Quintus, for himself and his parents, and his uncle Hilarus, freedman of Trypo.
EDCS-12400495.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Quintus Caesutius Trypo for himself, his parents, and his uncle.

EDCS-12400496.translation: Faustus, son of Caesutia, Philoca, freedwoman of Quintus, his mother.
EDCS-12400496.summary: This inscription identifies Faustus and his mother Philoca, likely as part of a funerary monument.

EDCS-12400497.translation: To the departed spirits. Cesia daughter to her mother Celsia Felicia, erected this monument.
EDCS-12400497.summary: This inscription records a daughter, Cesia, erecting a monument for her deceased mother, Celsia Felicia.

EDCS-12400498.translation: Erecia Rufa, daughter of Marcus, for herself and her children. ERE
EDCS-12400498.summary: This inscription records Erecia Rufa dedicating something for herself and her children, possibly a funerary monument.

EDCS-12400499.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Traebatius Fortunatus and his son [erected this] for his deserving wife Octavia Laliana.
EDCS-12400499.summary: This inscription records a husband and son dedicating a monument to a deceased wife and mother, Octavia Laliana.

EDCS-12400500.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording the name and possibly the lifespan of Publius Murrius Suavis.

EDCS-12400501.translation: To the spirits of the departed, the sons made this tomb for their pious and most loving father Sabinus.
EDCS-12400501.summary: A tomb built by sons for their father, Sabinus.

EDCS-12400502.translation: To Marcus Paccius, son of Caius, of the Galeria tribe, a primipilaris of the Fourth Scythian Legion.
EDCS-12400502.summary: An inscription honoring Marcus Paccius, a primipilaris of the Fourth Scythian Legion.

EDCS-12400503.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Publius Oppius Galeria Marcellinus, splendid Roman knight, grandson of Publius, great-grandson of Publius, great-great-grandson of Publius, curator of several communities, leader of the colony of Aeclanum, patron of the Compsini, Fratuentini, and Neretini, made this for himself and for his dearest wife Eppia Firma, and for his grandson Tettaeus Marcellinus, while alive.
EDCS-12400503.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Publius Oppius Galeria Marcellinus, a prominent local official and patron, for himself, his wife, and grandson, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12400504.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Publius Oppius Marcellinus made this for himself and for Junia Fortunata, his most loving wife.
EDCS-12400504.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Publius Oppius Marcellinus for himself and his wife, Junia Fortunata.

EDCS-12400506.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Publius Coius Rufinus, who lived 58 years, 7 months, and 15 days, buried on the sixth day before the Ides of October, in the consulship of Datianus and Cerialis.
EDCS-12400506.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Coius Rufinus, dating to the consulship of Datianus and Cerialis (likely 174 AD).

EDCS-12400507.translation: To Marcus Baebius Flaonianus Maximianus. Marcus Baebius Flaonianus Maximianus made this for his dearest son, who lived 4 years, 10 months, and 15 days, buried 18 days after his death. If anyone opens it, he will pay 500 sesterces to the treasury.
EDCS-12400507.summary: A funerary monument for Marcus Baebius Flaonianus Maximianus, erected by his father, with a penalty for grave robbers.

EDCS-12400508.translation: Marcus Palius, son of Caius, made this for himself.
EDCS-12400508.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Marcus Palius, son of Caius, for himself.

EDCS-12400509.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Fad Firmanus made this for Caesias Jucunda, his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-12400509.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Fad Firmanus for his wife, Caesias Jucunda.

EDCS-12400510.translation: Marcus Mindius Hirpinus, son of Marcus, made this for his well-deserving wife Betitia Secunda, freedwoman of Caius, and for his son Mindius Hirpinus, and for his dearest mother Julia Restituta. L.E. [meaning unclear]
EDCS-12400510.summary: A funerary monument erected by Marcus Mindius Hirpinus for his wife, son, and mother, likely during the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12400511.translation: Lucius Seppius Paullus, husband, made this for Ennia Restituta, daughter of Quintus.
EDCS-12400511.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Lucius Seppius Paullus for his wife, Ennia Restituta.

EDCS-12400512.translation: Gaius Pontius, son of Maraeus, is buried here.
EDCS-12400512.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the burial of Gaius Pontius.

EDCS-12400513.translation: Gaius Blassius, son of Caius, of the Galerian tribe, and Publius Blassius, son of Caius, made this for themselves and their families while they were alive.
EDCS-12400513.summary: This is a tombstone inscription by Gaius and Publius Blassius, making provision for their families.

EDCS-12400514.translation: Marcus Eppius, son of Quintus, of the Galerian tribe, died at the age of 23.
EDCS-12400514.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Marcus Eppius at the age of 23.

EDCS-12400516.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Trebius Maximus, son of Marcus.
EDCS-12400516.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the spirits of the underworld for Marcus Trebius Maximus.

EDCS-12400518.translation: Avius Apreus made this monument or tomb for his comrade Aplavita, and it will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-12400518.summary: This monument was erected by Avius Apreus for his comrade Aplavita, with specific instructions about inheritance.

EDCS-12400520.translation: Resia Victoria willingly paid her vow to the Eternal God.
EDCS-12400520.summary: This is a votive inscription by Resia Victoria, fulfilling a vow to a deity.

EDCS-12400521.translation: Marcus Fulvius Flaccus, son of Marcus; Gaius Sempronius Gracchus, son of Tiberius; Gaius Paperius Carbo, son of Caius, as a board of three men for judging and assigning lands, the former possessor's land.
EDCS-12400521.summary: This inscription records the actions of a triumviral commission led by Flaccus, Gracchus, and Carbo regarding land distribution.

EDCS-12400522.translation: Marcus Fulvius Flaccus, son of Marcus; Gaius Sempronius Gracchus, son of Tiberius; Gaius Paperius Carbo, son of Caius, as a board of three men for judging and assigning lands, land granted to new settlers.
EDCS-12400522.summary: This inscription, likely related to the previous one, details land allocation to new settlers by the same triumviral commission.

EDCS-12400524.translation: Publius Maius, son of Marcus, and Publius Allius, son of Marcus, as prefects, saw to the construction of the sun-dials.
EDCS-12400524.summary: This inscription records that two prefects, Publius Maius and Publius Allius, were responsible for the construction of sundials.

EDCS-12400525.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Cornelius Restutus set this up for his well-deserving wife, Aelia Crescentiana.
EDCS-12400525.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Cornelius Restutus for his wife, Aelia Crescentiana.

EDCS-12400527.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Vettius Quintianus made this for his deserving wife, Iulia Felicissima.
EDCS-12400527.summary: A funerary dedication by a husband for his wife.

EDCS-12400528.translation: Publius Minatius Silvinus, freedman of Ida.
EDCS-12400528.summary: A personal inscription identifying an individual and his former status, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-12400530.translation: Severus made this for his deserving protector Ebinus.
EDCS-12400530.summary: A dedication from Severus to his deserving protector Ebinus.

EDCS-12400531.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Spedia Maximina made this for her deserving patron, Caius Spedius Felix.
EDCS-12400531.summary: A funerary dedication by a freedwoman for her patron.

EDCS-12400533.translation: Murria, daughter of Publius, of the Pollian tribe.
EDCS-12400533.summary: An identification of a woman by her father's name and tribal affiliation, possibly a funerary marker or property record.

EDCS-12400535.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. [He/She] made this monument for Firmianus Potius, [his/her] spouse.
EDCS-12400535.summary: A funerary dedication for a spouse, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400536.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Spedia Felicissima made this for her deserving husband, Lucudeius Festinus.
EDCS-12400536.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife for her husband.

EDCS-12400537.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintia made this monument for herself and her family.
EDCS-12400537.summary: A funerary inscription where an individual dedicates a monument for herself and her relatives.

EDCS-12400538.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Epidia Successa made this for her deserving husband, Cacelius Maximus.
EDCS-12400538.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife for her husband.

EDCS-12400540.translation: Publius Marcius Suettlus, son of Marcus; Marcia, his sister; Faustus, his freedman, took care to have this made.
EDCS-12400540.summary: A funerary inscription detailing the responsibilities of surviving family members and a freedman for the deceased Publius Marcius Suettlus.

EDCS-12400541.translation: To the Divine Shades. Varronia Via dedicated this to I. Varronius Atimetus the Younger.
EDCS-12400541.summary: This is a funerary dedication to a man named I. Varronius Atimetus the Younger by Varronia Via.

EDCS-12400542.translation: His wife erected this to Con gonus, who deserved well. [---]
EDCS-12400542.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife to her deserving husband, Con gonus.

EDCS-12400547.translation: Caius Trebius Galeria Nepos [dedicated this] for himself and [---] in the exercise of his judicial duties.
EDCS-12400547.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Caius Trebius Galeria Nepos, likely a magistrate, for himself and perhaps his family.

EDCS-12400556.summary: This is a brief epitaph or identification of Caius Antistius Isochrysus, an architect.

EDCS-12400558.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely an epitaph or identification for Aurelius Crastinus and Claudia Ianuaria.

EDCS-12400559.translation: Caprius and Oneris, father and mother, erected this to their most holy, most sweet son, Caprio, who deserved well.
EDCS-12400559.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by parents, Caprius and Oneris, to their beloved son, Caprio.

EDCS-12400560.translation: Quintus Cornelius, son of Quintus, of the Stellatina tribe; Cornelia, freedwoman of Quintus, his wife; Quintus Cornelius Maximus, their son; Cornelia Tertia, their daughter. [---]
EDCS-12400560.summary: This is a family epitaph listing Quintus Cornelius, his wife Cornelia, and their children Quintus Cornelius Maximus and Cornelia Tertia.

EDCS-12400562.translation: Publius Ferennius Firmus, son of Publius, aged 18 years.
EDCS-12400562.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Ferennius Firmus, who died at the age of 18.

EDCS-12400563.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Aretas, his spouse, and [---].
EDCS-12400563.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Aretas, the spouse of the dedicator, with incomplete additional information.

EDCS-12400565.translation: Lucius Pr us Phileros [lived] 23 years, 3 months, and 15 days. Vibulcae Cidippe [dedicated this to] them.
EDCS-12400565.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Lucius Pr us Phileros and indicates it was dedicated by Vibulcae Cidippe.

EDCS-12400566.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Sallustia Capreola. Sallustia Dextra made this for her well-deserving one.
EDCS-12400566.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from the Imperial period, records Sallustia Dextra dedicating a monument to Sallustia Capreola.

EDCS-12400567.translation: Secundus, son of Marcus. C. C. O. V. M. S.
EDCS-12400567.summary: This very fragmentary inscription, possibly dating to the Roman Republic or early Imperial period, appears to be a personal dedication or epitaph with unclear abbreviations.

EDCS-12400569.translation: Trebia, daughter of Quintus, made this.
EDCS-12400569.summary: This simple dedication, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records Trebia, daughter of Quintus, as the maker of the monument.

EDCS-12400573.translation: To Dulcis, the soul of Probus, who lived about 13 years. His mother Vensilde, with Vitus Adon, an assistant deacon, and Martina, with the subdeacons, made this for her most beloved one on October 2nd, in the 52nd year [of his life], well-deserving.
EDCS-12400573.summary: This funerary inscription, probably from the later Roman Empire, commemorates Probus, who died at approximately 13 years old, with dedications from his mother and other church officials.

EDCS-12400577.translation: Here rests in the sleep of peace Gaudiosus, who lived about 4 years. His deposition was on the 5th day before the Kalends of September, in the consulship of Flavius Severinus, most distinguished man.
EDCS-12400577.summary: This funerary inscription, from the late Roman Empire, records the burial and lifespan of Gaudiosus, noting his death date and the consul Flavius Severinus.

EDCS-12400580.summary: This short inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, is a dedication to Publius Dextrius.

EDCS-12400584.translation: Here rests in the sleep of peace Claudius Dulcitius, primicerius, who lived about 30 years. Deposition.
EDCS-12400584.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from the late Roman Empire, records the death and approximate age of Claudius Dulcitius, who held the rank of primicerius.

EDCS-12400591.summary: This simple dedication, possibly from the Roman Imperial period, is made to Titus Annius Verus.

EDCS-12400592.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Sattia Silvina, a most chaste woman, to whom I always believed myself unequal in my anxieties, with whom I lived for 32 years, 10 months, and 10 days in the same labor, for indeed I cannot give enough thanks for my grief for her, as much as I did when she was alive, who had nothing dearer than her husband and children, who, when she was alive, even if she had reached the security of myself or our children...
EDCS-12400592.summary: This deeply personal and emotional funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, is dedicated to Sattia Silvina by her grieving husband, detailing their long marriage and her devotion.

EDCS-12400598.translation: Sacred to the Genius of this place.
EDCS-12400598.summary: This short dedication, possibly from the Roman Imperial period, is a dedication to the genius loci (guardian spirit of a place).

EDCS-12400608.summary: A very brief inscription likely referring to Bassus, possibly a military figure or official, and indicating his origin or area of command.

EDCS-12400621.translation: To our lord Flavius Constantius, pious, fortunate, ever Augustus, who is subjugating the world to the Roman name, Annius Antiochus, a most perfect man, corrector of Apulia and Calabria, ever devoted.
EDCS-12400621.summary: A dedication to Emperor Constantius by Annius Antiochus, a high-ranking official (corrector) of the provinces of Apulia and Calabria, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-12400632.translation: The council of the city of Aeclanum has decreed that a statue be erected to Umbonius Mannachius, a most distinguished man, a most worthy patron, who, with his great contribution to our city, adding much to its utility and adornment, is most outstanding in his unique merits, industry, and eloquence towards our city.
EDCS-12400632.summary: A decree from the city council of Aeclanum to erect a statue honoring Umbonius Mannachius for his distinguished service and contributions to the city, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400642.translation: Gaius Obellius, son of Gaius, and Gaius Marius, son of Gaius, the IIII viri iure dicundo, by decree of the senate, have seen to the construction of a crepidinem (a step or base) with their own funds.
EDCS-12400642.summary: An inscription by two magistrates (IIII viri iure dicundo) of a municipality, recording the construction of a base or step at public expense, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400644.translation: Gaius Quinctius Valgus, son of Gaius, patron of the municipality, and Marcus Magius Surus, son of Minatius, and Aulus Patlacius, son of Quintus, the IIII viri, by decree of the senate, have seen to the construction of the gates, towers, and equal walls with the gates.
EDCS-12400644.summary: An inscription by four magistrates (IIII viri) of a municipality, detailing the construction of city gates, towers, and walls, funded by public decree, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400678.translation: [?] son of Nerenius, [son of?] Lupercus, flamen.
EDCS-12400678.summary: A fragmented inscription naming members of the Nerenii family and identifying one as a flamen, suggesting a religious or civic official in a local context, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400695.translation: [Names including] Maianicus Taurus, Plaetorius Salutaris, Ofilius Epitynchanus, Ofilius Celer, Ofilius Aster, Ofilius Felix, Attius, Maianicus, Ofilius, Trebatius.
EDCS-12400695.summary: A list of names, possibly representing a group, officials, or members of a collegium, with several individuals bearing the name Ofilius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400696.translation: Eutychus, Saturninus, Socellianus, Romanus, Apollodorus, Epitinchanus, Primitivus, Crispinus, Secundus, Tertius, Felix. To [?]
EDCS-12400696.summary: A list of names, likely members of a group or association, with Greek-sounding cognomina, possibly from a collegium or funerary context, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400717.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Ambrosius, his son, for his own thrift, from Aeclania.
EDCS-12400717.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Ambrosius by his son, highlighting his thriftiness, from the city of Aeclania, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400720.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Lucius Annius Maximilianus and Aecclania Magna.
EDCS-12400720.summary: A joint funerary inscription for Lucius Annius Maximilianus and Aecclania Magna, likely a married couple or family members, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400743.translation: Son of God and Caesellia Paul
EDCS-12400743.summary: This inscription fragment likely refers to a dedication to a deity and individuals named Caesellia Paul, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400748.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Caucia Felicula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400761.summary: This inscription fragment names Erotine, a freedwoman of Quintus, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400768.summary: This inscription fragment simply records the name Lucius Furius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400784.summary: This inscription fragment is a dedication to Marcus Magius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400815.translation: To Numerius Staius Remissus, freedman of Numerius. Marcus Luccius Marcellus [made this].
EDCS-12400815.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Marcus Luccius Marcellus to his freedman Numerius Staius Remissus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12400867.translation: In memory of Eggius Leon, who lived 24 years, 5 months, buried on the Kalends of September after the consulship of the most distinguished Theodore; [she] made [this] for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-12400867.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Eggius Leon, who died at age 24 years and 5 months, buried on the Kalends of September, with the dedication made by her husband after the consulship of Theodore, likely from the late Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-12400869.translation: For the good memory of Aurelia Crispinna, his most sweet wife. Crescentianus had this made, buried on the 12th day before the Kalends of August, in the second consulship after Honorius VIII and Theodosius III Augusti.
EDCS-12400869.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Crescentianus to his wife Aurelia Crispinna, dated to the second consulship after Honorius VIII and Theodosius III Augusti, likely from the late Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-12400871.translation: For the good memory of Vibrio Iustinus, who lived 40 years, 7 days. The burial of this man was on the 4th day before the Kalends of September, when our lord V and the most distinguished Anatholius were consuls.
EDCS-12400871.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Vibrio Iustinus, who lived 40 years and 7 days, with his burial on the 4th day before the Kalends of September during the consulship of 'our lord V' and Anatholius, likely from the late Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-12400872.translation: For the good memory of Caia Eppia Taecia, his most sweet wife, who lived 17 years, 5 months, 13 days. Her burial was on the 2nd day before the Ides of January, after the second consulship of Petronius Maximus, the most distinguished man. Her husband SAPIENTVS SA ERIA [made this].
EDCS-12400872.summary: This is a funerary inscription by the husband, Sapiens Saeria, to his wife Caia Eppia Taecia, who died at age 17 years, 5 months, and 13 days, buried on the 2nd day before the Ides of January after the second consulship of Petronius Maximus, likely from the late Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-12400874.translation: To the good memory of Victorinus, dearest husband, who lived for more or less 80 years. His burial was on the 5th day before the Kalends of December, during the consulship of our lord Valentinian VII and the most distinguished Avienus. Lea made this with her family.
EDCS-12400874.summary: A funerary inscription for Victorinus, set up by his wife Lea, with a precise date in the consulship of Valentinian VII and Avienus.

EDCS-12400876.translation: To the spirits of the dead. Vivius Marcianus rests here in the sleep of peace, who lived for more or less 70 years. His burial was on the day before the Kalends of February, after the consulship of the most distinguished Recemedis.
EDCS-12400876.summary: A funerary inscription for Vivius Marcianus, dated by the post-consulate of Recemedis.

EDCS-12400877.translation: Victorina rests here, who lived 5 years, 2 months, and 15 days. Her burial was on the Ides of May, during the first consulship of our lord Severus Augustus.
EDCS-12400877.summary: A funerary inscription for Victorina, detailing her age and burial date during the first consulship of Emperor Severus.

EDCS-12400879.translation: Felix rests here in the sleep of peace, who lived for more or less 40 years. His burial was on the 12th day before the Kalends of February, during the consulship of the most distinguished Flavius Venantius.
EDCS-12400879.summary: A funerary inscription for Felix, dated by the consulship of Flavius Venantius.

EDCS-12400880.translation: Fortunata rests here, who lived 60 years. Her burial was on the 18th day before the Kalends of May, after the consulship of the most distinguished Probus Faustus. Of good memory.
EDCS-12400880.summary: A funerary inscription for Fortunata, dated by the post-consulate of Probus Faustus.

EDCS-12400881.translation: Morasius rests here, who lived 55 years. His burial was on the 3rd day before the Kalends of September, during the consulship of the most distinguished Flavius Cethegus.
EDCS-12400881.summary: A funerary inscription for Morasius, dated by the consulship of Flavius Cethegus.

EDCS-12400882.translation: Caelis Laurentius, lector of the holy church of Aeclanum, rests here in the sleep of peace, who lived for more or less 49 years. His burial was on the 8th day before the Ides of May, during the consulship of the most distinguished Flavius Asterius and Praesidius.
EDCS-12400882.summary: A funerary inscription for Caelis Laurentius, a lector of the church of Aeclanum, dated by the consulship of Flavius Asterius and Praesidius.

EDCS-12400883.translation: Iulius, a man of standing, rests here in the sleep of peace, who lived for more or less 60 years. His burial was on the day before the Ides of November, during the consulship of the most distinguished Venantius the Younger. His sweetest wife, the lady Petiliana, a woman of standing, made this for her husband.
EDCS-12400883.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulius, set up by his wife Petiliana, dated by the consulship of Venantius the Younger.

EDCS-12400885.translation: Of good memory. Fulgentia rests here in the sleep of peace, who lived for more or less 40 years. Her burial was on the 2nd day before the Nones of February, during the consulship of the most distinguished Flavius Felix.
EDCS-12400885.summary: A funerary inscription for Fulgentia, dated by the consulship of Flavius Felix.

EDCS-12400886.translation: Caelis Iohannis, exorcist, rests here in the sleep of peace, who lived for more or less 20 years. His burial was on the 2nd day of the Ides of December, during the consulship of the most distinguished Flavius Felix.
EDCS-12400886.summary: A funerary inscription for Caelis Iohannis, an exorcist, dated by the consulship of Flavius Felix.

EDCS-12400887.translation: Agrestia Spes, who lived about 70 years, rests here in the sleep of peace; her burial was on the third day before the Kalends of November, when Florentinus, a most distinguished man, was consul.
EDCS-12400887.summary: This inscription records the burial of Agrestia Spes, who lived approximately 70 years, with her burial date recorded under the consulship of Florentinus.

EDCS-12400888.translation: Veronilla, a distinguished woman, who lived 80 years and 6 months, rests here in the sleep of peace; her burial was on the sixth day before the Nones of November, when Agapitus, a most distinguished man, was consul.
EDCS-12400888.summary: This inscription records the burial of Veronilla, a distinguished woman, who lived 80 years and 6 months, under the consulship of Agapitus.

EDCS-12400889.translation: Fuscus, who lived about 10 years, rests here in the sleep of peace; his burial was on the third day of the Nones of June, when Flavius Decius the Younger was consul. Lucentius, who lived about 60 years, rests here; his burial was on the thirteenth day before the Kalends of January, and again after the consulship of Lampadius and Orestes.
EDCS-12400889.summary: This inscription records the burials of Fuscus, who lived about 10 years, and Lucentius, who lived about 60 years, with dates specified under the consulship of Flavius Decius the Younger and post-consular references.

EDCS-12400890.translation: Murrasius, an acolyte, who lived about 35 years, rests here in the sleep of peace, of good memory; his burial was on the Kalends of September, when Flavius Decius the Younger, a most distinguished man, was consul.
EDCS-12400890.summary: This inscription records the burial of Murrasius, an acolyte who lived about 35 years, under the consulship of Flavius Decius the Younger.

EDCS-12400891.translation: Marcianus, of good memory, who lived about 15 years, rests here in the sleep of peace; his burial was on the Kalends of January, when Flavius John was consul.
EDCS-12400891.summary: This inscription records the burial of Marcianus, who lived about 15 years, with his burial date noted under the consulship of Flavius John.

EDCS-12400893.translation: Maria, who lived about 65 years, rests here in the sleep of peace; her burial was on the fourth day of the Nones of January, five years after the consulship of Basilius, a most distinguished man, in the 10th indiction.
EDCS-12400893.summary: This inscription records the burial of Maria, who lived about 65 years, with her burial date specified relative to the consulship of Basilius and the 10th indiction.

EDCS-12400895.translation: To the divine shades. For Baebius Capriolus, dearest son, who lived 8 years and 2 months, a most dear son of good memory. His father Leopardus caused this to be made.
EDCS-12400895.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Leopardus to his son Baebius Capriolus, who died at the age of 8 years and 2 months.

EDCS-12400896.translation: Of good memory. Biria Iulia, who lived about 35 years, buried on the eleventh day before the Kalends of October. Casser Maiis L A.
EDCS-12400896.summary: This inscription records the burial of Biria Iulia, who lived about 35 years, with her burial date on the eleventh day before the Kalends of October.

EDCS-12400898.translation: Iuliana, the prefect, who lived 54 years, rests here in the sleep of peace; her burial was on the eighth day before the Kalends of January.
EDCS-12400898.summary: This inscription records the burial of Iuliana, identified as a prefect, who lived 54 years, with her burial date on the eighth day before the Kalends of January.

EDCS-12400911.translation: To the divine shades. Quintus Messius Geminus made this for his well-deserving wife, Platoria Casta.
EDCS-12400911.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Quintus Messius Geminus to his deserving wife, Platoria Casta.

EDCS-12400912.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ovia dedicated this monument, with her own money, to her deserving father, Lucius Rupilianus.
EDCS-12400912.summary: A funerary dedication by Ovia to her father, Lucius Rupilianus.

EDCS-12400913.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Fonteia Ianuaria set up this monument to her sweetest deserving son, Fonteius Marcellinus, on the ninth.
EDCS-12400913.summary: A funerary dedication by Fonteia Ianuaria to her son, Fonteius Marcellinus.

EDCS-12400915.summary: The name of Aelius Ianuarius, likely a funerary epitaph.

EDCS-12400916.summary: The name of Marcus Ianuarius, likely a funerary epitaph.

EDCS-12400918.translation: Lucius Salvius Agato, son of Lucius, and Philemation, daughter of Publius, freedwoman of Publius, [dedicated this monument] to Publius Patulcius Bassus.
EDCS-12400918.summary: A dedication by Lucius Salvius Agato and Philemation to Publius Patulcius Bassus.

EDCS-12400925.translation: Saturninus, a freedman of our emperor, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-12400925.summary: A votive offering by Saturninus, a freedman of the emperor, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-12400928.summary: A possessive inscription, likely indicating ownership of a tomb or property by Quintus Hostrius Silvinus.

EDCS-12400930.translation: Labia Firma, daughter of Caius, made this for herself and for Marcus Seius Marci filius Cornelia Maximus, a deserving son and decurion of Aeclanum, who lived 20 years, 8 months.
EDCS-12400930.summary: A self-dedication and dedication to a son by Labia Firma, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-12400932.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Maximae, wife of Marcus Allius Marcellus, made this for her deserving wife.
EDCS-12400932.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Allius Marcellus to his deserving wife, Maximae.

EDCS-12400938.translation: Marcus Caedius, son of Caius, of the Galeria tribe, is buried here.
EDCS-12400938.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Caedius, son of Caius, of the Galeria tribe.

EDCS-12400939.translation: To the Divine Manes. Sabidia Rufina dedicated this to her well-deserving husband, Marcus Cleppius Maximinius.
EDCS-12400939.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a wife to her husband, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400940.translation: Marcus Decumius Apio, freedman of Marcus, is buried here.
EDCS-12400940.summary: A tombstone marking the burial place of a freedman, dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400941.translation: Gavillius Priscianus dedicated this to his well-deserving wife, Fufia Maximilla.
EDCS-12400941.summary: A funerary inscription set up by a husband for his wife, probable from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400942.translation: Caius Iulius Ianuarius willingly placed this memorial to Atria, with whom he lived for 16 years and 6 months.
EDCS-12400942.summary: A memorial stone erected by a husband for his deceased partner, indicating a long-term relationship, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400944.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucius Turselius Priscus dedicated this to his dear, well-deserving Marcia Saturnina, with whom he lived for 25 years.
EDCS-12400944.summary: A funerary epitaph for a wife, set up by her husband who also records the duration of their marriage, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400945.translation: Maria Romanilla, mother, dedicated this to her most dutiful son, Mari Liberalis, who lived 17 years.
EDCS-12400945.summary: A funerary dedication by a mother for her deceased son, probably from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400947.translation: To the Divine Manes. Octavia Rufina and her sons unwillingly dedicated this to Caius Ofilius Gemellus, an incomparable man.
EDCS-12400947.summary: A funerary monument erected by a wife and sons for an 'incomparable' husband and father, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400948.translation: Manius Oppius Daphnus, father, and Caerellia Proba, mother, saw to the construction for their son Daphnus, who lived 19 years, 4 months, and 10 days.
EDCS-12400948.summary: A funerary commission by parents for their deceased son, including precise age at death, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400949.translation: To the Divine Manes. Platius Secundus and Iulia Rufa dedicated this to their sweetest and most dutiful daughter, Platia Rufina, who lived 20 years and 8 months.
EDCS-12400949.summary: A funerary monument set up by parents for their deceased daughter, providing her age at death, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400951.translation: Publius Salluvius Rufus, son of Publius, and Marcus Salluvius Cogitatus, son of Publius, arranged for this monument or tomb to be built for their father, Publius Salluvius Rufus, and their mother, Italia, freedwoman of Publius, as a memorial of their dutiful love as sons.
EDCS-12400951.summary: A tomb commissioned by two sons for their father and mother, highlighting filial piety, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400953.translation: To Severa, Mamius Ianuarius made this for his most holy and incomparably beloved wife, with whom he lived for 13 years, 5 months, and 20 days, who deserved well.
EDCS-12400953.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Mamius Ianuarius for his wife Severa, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12400954.translation: Monument for Statia Heliena, a freedwoman, for herself, her husband, and her family.
EDCS-12400954.summary: This funerary monument was set up for Statia Heliena and her family, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12400964.translation: The guild of woodworkers and the guild of builders, contributing funds, erected this for their patron, Caius Amarfius Saturninus, son of Quintus, veteran of our Augusts, former decurion in the Second Parthian Legion and holder of all honors, father and grandfather of decurions, because of his exceptional affection towards individuals and citizens alike, and for his most benevolent patronage; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-12400964.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by guilds to honor Caius Amarfius Saturninus, a veteran and former decurion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400965.translation: Caius Valerius Arsaces, son of Caius, of the Aemilia tribe, from the Fifth Legion Alaudae, ordered this to be made by his will for himself and for Valeria Urbana, freedwoman of Caius, his concubine.
EDCS-12400965.summary: This funerary monument was commissioned by Caius Valerius Arsaces for himself and his concubine Valeria Urbana, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12400966.translation: Caius Clodius Lucifer, freedman of Caius, an Augustalis, and Vibunia Uri, freedwoman of Lucius, his wife, made this for themselves, and for Clodia, daughter of Idulius; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-12400966.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Caius Clodius Lucifer and his wife Vibunia Uri for themselves and their daughter Clodia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400967.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Irvinia Sabina made this for her well-deserving husband, Caius Lisidius Modestus, decurion.
EDCS-12400967.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Irvinia Sabina to her husband, Caius Lisidius Modestus, a decurion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400968.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. The guild of woodworkers made this for their well-deserving colleague Titus Petronius Ponticus, and [for] Pontica, the most unhappy father [dated] XI.
EDCS-12400968.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by the guild of woodworkers for their colleague Titus Petronius Ponticus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400969.translation: Lucius Pullius Mactorianus, son of Lucius, from his own money.
EDCS-12400969.summary: This inscription indicates that Lucius Pullius Mactorianus paid for something from his own funds, likely a funerary monument or public work, dating from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400970.translation: Lucius Turselius Rufinus, son of Lucius, of the Velina tribe, made this for his paternal grandfather, Lucius Turselius, son of Lucius, of the Velina tribe, pontiff, aedile, quaestor, twice chief magistrate, and for his paternal grandmother, Voconia Procula, freedwoman of Lucius, who deserved well.
EDCS-12400970.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Lucius Turselius Rufinus for his paternal grandparents, detailing their public careers, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400972.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Numerius Ces[...]. Numerius Ces[...].
EDCS-12400972.summary: This is a fragmented funerary dedication to Numerius Ces[...], likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400973.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Felicula, unhappy mother, made this for her deserving son, Lucius Cornelius Modestus.
EDCS-12400973.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a mother for her son, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12400974.translation: By testament of Publius, freedman of Davus, under the supervision of Primi, freedman.
EDCS-12400974.summary: This inscription records the execution of a testament concerning a freedman, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-12400975.translation: Camuria Primitiva for her husband Numerius Didius Felicissimus, with whom she lived for 11 years and X months, a deserving man.
EDCS-12400975.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her husband, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12400976.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Epiacharidis, a deserving fellow slave.
EDCS-12400976.summary: This is a funerary dedication to a fellow slave, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12400977.translation: Quintus Fabius Tertius and Fabricia Nice, mother, and Peregrinus are buried here.
EDCS-12400977.summary: This is a funerary inscription identifying individuals buried in a location, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12400978.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Cornelius Vitalis made this for his deserving wife, Gavia Gemella.
EDCS-12400978.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a husband for his wife, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12400979.translation: Herennius Felix made this for his deserving daughter, Octavia.
EDCS-12400979.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a father for his daughter, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12400980.translation: For Lucius Irvinius, a citizen of the city.
EDCS-12400980.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Irvinius, identifying him as a city citizen, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12400981.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Primitianus, his ward, made this for his deserving Julia Hecate.
EDCS-12400981.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a ward for his patron or guardian, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12400982.translation: Gavelius Liberalis made this for his deserving wife, Liccia Myssis.
EDCS-12400982.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a husband for his wife, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-12400983.translation: To the Divine Spirits. A son made this for his most excellent parents, Lucius Mettius Patientis, son of Lucius, of the Trebulan tribe, and Iusta, daughter of Quintus.
EDCS-12400983.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a son for his parents.

EDCS-12400984.translation: Marcus Octavius made this for himself and his wife Maioria during his lifetime.
EDCS-12400984.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Marcus Octavius for himself and his wife.

EDCS-12400985.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Tintirius Asianus made this for his well-deserving father, Petilius Asianus.
EDCS-12400985.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring a father, dedicated by his son.

EDCS-12400986.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Quintus Petronius Ampliatus and Camuria Priscilla made this for their well-deserving son, Quintus Petronius Ianuarius, who lived 19 years.
EDCS-12400986.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by parents for their son who died at age 19.

EDCS-12400987.translation: Procne made this for herself and her beloved (pet) SAC, and for her wife, Publius Flaminius Urbanus, son of Caius.
EDCS-12400987.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Publius Flaminius Urbanus to his wife and his pet.

EDCS-12400988.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Caius Quintius Proculus, father, and Sabina, mother, made this for their dearest son, Caius Quintius Severus.
EDCS-12400988.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by parents for their beloved son.

EDCS-12400989.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Traius Honoratus, son of Lucius, made this for his most excellent mother, Quintia Phoebas.
EDCS-12400989.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a son to his mother.

EDCS-12400990.translation: To the Divine Spirits below. Sabini and Iucunda made this for their most unfortunate son, dedicated to the Republic of the Ligurians.
EDCS-12400990.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by parents to their son and related to the Republic of Ligurians.

EDCS-12400991.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Tiberius Statorius Felicissimus made this for my wife, with whom he lived 40 years.
EDCS-12400991.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Tiberius Statorius Felicissimus for his wife, commemorating their 40 years of marriage.

EDCS-12400992.translation: To the Divine Spirits below. Cnaeus Suellius Eros, freedman of Cnaeus Suellius, placed this.
EDCS-12400992.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a freedman for himself.

EDCS-12400993.translation: To the divine spirits. Gaius Crispius Salutaris made this for Tigrinus, his well-deserving beloved, with whom he lived for 17 years and 10 months.
EDCS-12400993.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a man for his male beloved, recording their relationship and duration.

EDCS-12400994.translation: Turselia II? A. A. For Lucius Turselius Ant., son of Publius Verginnius Reginus, for Lucius Turselius, son of Optatus Saturninus, and Vitalis.
EDCS-12400994.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary dedication or memorial, possibly for members of the Turselia family and associated individuals.

EDCS-12400995.translation: To the divine spirits. For Turselia Prima, his most chaste wife and incomparable woman. Lucius Petronius Geminianus, her husband, made this for her, the well-deserving, with whom he lived for 28 years and 22 days.
EDCS-12400995.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by a husband for his deceased wife, praising her virtues and recording their marriage duration.

EDCS-12400996.translation: Regilla, freedwoman of Turselia, for herself and for Lucius Tullius, son of Lucius, of the Velina tribe, and for her Storgene, and for Tursellius Gamalus, 12 feet in front and 15 feet in the field.
EDCS-12400996.summary: This inscription records a property division or boundary marker, with a freedwoman dedicating space for herself and others, including a freedman.

EDCS-12400997.translation: A grieving mother... for the common slave, lived.
EDCS-12400997.summary: This fragmented inscription likely refers to a slave and a grieving owner, possibly a funerary dedication.

EDCS-12400998.translation: ... made for Fausto, his father, as much as is allowed by the gods.
EDCS-12400998.summary: This is a dedication or funerary monument made by a son for his father, with a religious invocation.

EDCS-12400999.translation: This deity... for Badia, the well-deserving.
EDCS-12400999.summary: This fragmented inscription is likely a dedication to a deity or a funerary epitaph for a person named Badia.

EDCS-12401001.summary: This inscription appears to be a warning or prohibition related to access, possibly concerning property or a public space.

EDCS-12401004.translation: To the all-powerful god Jupiter. Publius Staius Eutychianus [dedicated this].
EDCS-12401004.summary: This is a religious dedication to Jupiter, made by Publius Staius Eutychianus.

EDCS-12401008.translation: Titus Antonius, son of Publius, of the Stellatina tribe, a soldier in the 5th legion, and Origo, freedwoman of Secundus, his concubine, and Antonia, freedwoman of Titus.
EDCS-12401008.summary: This inscription lists individuals, including a soldier, his concubine, and a freedwoman, possibly related to a funerary monument or family record.

EDCS-12401010.translation: Cnaeus Suellius Eutyches: a place was granted by decree of the town councillors, with seats.
EDCS-12401010.summary: This inscription records the granting of a public space with seating, likely for public use, by the town councillors. The approximate date is inferable from the style and content as likely Imperial Roman.

EDCS-12401011.translation: To the Divine Manes, for Verecundus, by the collegium.
EDCS-12401011.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a collegium (association or guild) to one of its deceased members, Verecundus.

EDCS-12401012.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Marcus Cosinius Priscus, a pork seller. Tuccia Prima, his wife, with whom he lived for 45 years, and Marcus Cosinius Priscus and Marcus Cosinius Priscianus, his sons, made this for their well-deserving father. He lies on his own land.
EDCS-12401012.summary: This is a family tomb inscription for Marcus Cosinius Priscus, a pork seller, set up by his wife and sons, indicating his burial on family property and likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401013.translation: Publius Aelius Balbus, freedman of Publius, made this for Petellia.
EDCS-12401013.summary: This inscription records that Publius Aelius Balbus, a freedman, commissioned this monument or burial place for Petellia, possibly a relative or freedwoman, and likely dates to the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-12401014.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Aurelius Callistus, who lived for 32 years. Gargonia Tigris, his well-deserving wife, made this.
EDCS-12401014.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife, Gargonia Tigris, to her deceased husband, Aurelius Callistus, specifying his age at death and indicating it's a tomb inscription from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401015.translation: Marcus Caecilius Galerus, son of Gaius, of the Galeria tribe, is buried here.
EDCS-12401015.summary: This is a simple burial inscription stating that Marcus Caecilius Galerus is interred at this location, with his name, father's name, and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-12401016.summary: This inscription is likely a marker or dedication belonging to Quintus Caesius Ianus, possibly indicating ownership or a memorial.

EDCS-12401017.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Publius Camurius Fortunatus and Polonia Modesta. Publius Camurinus Modestus, Fortunatus, and Quintina, their sons, made this for their most devoted parents, by agreement.
EDCS-12401017.summary: This funerary monument was erected by three children for their parents, Publius Camurius Fortunatus and Polonia Modesta, indicating a devoted family relationship and likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401018.translation: Kamui Optata made this for Caius Naevius.
EDCS-12401018.summary: This inscription records Kamui Optata commissioning a monument or grave marker for Caius Naevius.

EDCS-12401019.translation: Lived 72 years, 3 months, and 7 days. Camuria Prima and Camurius Primianus made this burial place for the deserving deceased.
EDCS-12401019.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of the deceased and notes that the burial plot was provided by Camuria Prima and Camurius Primianus for the deserving individual.

EDCS-12401022.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Curia Prisca made this for her deserving husband, Marcus Curius Tertius.
EDCS-12401022.summary: A funerary dedication made by a wife for her husband.

EDCS-12401023.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Her husband Delphicus made this for Ecathene, deserving.
EDCS-12401023.summary: A funerary dedication made by a husband for his wife.

EDCS-12401025.translation: To Lucius Iulius Urco, son of C Ximo, his brother.
EDCS-12401025.summary: A dedication, likely funerary, from a brother to Lucius Iulius Urco.

EDCS-12401026.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Luccius Maximus made this for his most excellent and deserving father, Caius Luccius Redemptus.
EDCS-12401026.summary: A funerary dedication made by a son for his father.

EDCS-12401027.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Nonius P. Municius made this for his deserving wife, Lucerina Iusta, who lived 32 years, 5 months, and 20 days, and with whom he lived 18 years and 8 months.
EDCS-12401027.summary: A funerary dedication by a husband for his wife, detailing her age and the duration of their marriage.

EDCS-12401028.translation: Caius Marcius, son of Caius, grandson of Quintus, from Mutro.
EDCS-12401028.summary: An inscription identifying an individual, Caius Marcius, likely on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-12401029.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Cosinius Priscus made this for his dearest and deserving Munatia Modestina, who lived 12 years.
EDCS-12401029.summary: A funerary dedication by a father (implied by naming convention) for his daughter, specifying her age.

EDCS-12401030.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Numerius Didius Felix made this for his deserving wife, Octavia Felicissima, who lived 46 years, 5 months, and with whom he lived 25 years, 4 months, and 3 days; I never profited from her chastity.
EDCS-12401030.summary: A funerary dedication by a husband for his wife, emphasizing her chastity and longevity, and the duration of their marriage.

EDCS-12401031.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Opimia Ianuaria made this for her deserving husband, Cnaeus Pompeius Modestus, with whom she lived 35 years.
EDCS-12401031.summary: A funerary dedication made by a wife for her husband, stating the length of their marriage.

EDCS-12401032.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Primio and Veneria made this for their deserving nurse, who lived 16 years and 2 months.
EDCS-12401032.summary: A funerary dedication made by children for their nurse, specifying her age.

EDCS-12401035.translation: Tituleia Felicissima made this for her incomparable husband, Lucius Traius Adiutor, to whom she owes much, and with whom she lived for 25 years.
EDCS-12401035.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a wife to her husband, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401036.translation: Caius Iulius Silvanus made this for his most virtuous wife, Veiania Rumna, to whom he owes much, and with whom he lived for 59 years without arguments or complaints.
EDCS-12401036.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a husband to his wife, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401037.translation: Caius Verginater made this for his son,nio, son of Caius.
EDCS-12401037.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a son, dedicated by his father, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401052.translation: Lucius Tarquinius Ianuarius fulfilled a vow to Hercules on his own land.
EDCS-12401052.summary: This is a votive inscription to Hercules, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401053.translation: Caius Falcilius, son of Lucius, consul, dedicated this sacred offering to the Quirites' Juno, [and] Ti. Iulius, son of Lucius, praetor.
EDCS-12401053.summary: This is a dedication to Juno Quirites by a consul and a praetor, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-12401058.translation: Plautia Felicitas gladly fulfilled a vow for her son Crescentinus to Silvanus Staianus.
EDCS-12401058.summary: This is a votive inscription to Silvanus Staianus, dedicated by a mother for her son, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401060.translation: During the consulship of Lucius Vinicius Quintus and Lucius Laronius, by order of Caius Varius Dentio, under the direction of Caius Varius Philippus, freedman of Caius, Pamphila [dedicated this] from her own funds.
EDCS-12401060.summary: This is a dedication possibly related to a public work or a private commission, mentioning specific magistrates and patrons, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-12401061.summary: This is a brief votive inscription, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12401064.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Optimus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 18th time, commander for the 7th time, consul for the 6th time, father of the fatherland, to the bravest prince, the Senate and the Roman People [dedicated this].
EDCS-12401064.summary: This is a grand honorific inscription dedicated to Emperor Trajan by the Senate and the Roman People, likely from his later reign (after 113 AD).

EDCS-12401067.translation: To Flavius Claudius Iulianus, most noble and most blessed Caesar, under the supervision of Tocius Maximus, a most distinguished man, curator of the public affairs of Beneventum.
EDCS-12401067.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated to a Caesar (likely a member of the imperial family or a high-ranking official) in Beneventum, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-12401068.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Valentis and Gratianus, likely from the late 4th century CE.

EDCS-12401069.translation: To our lords, the most invincible Emperors Theodosius and Valentinian. Aemilius Rufinus, a man of distinction, count of the first order, consular of Campania, arranged for the placement of this present statue, found in hidden places, in a very frequented public spot for decoration, with the urging of the praiseworthy man Firmus Euresi. May you always remain faithful to God.
EDCS-12401069.summary: This inscription records a statue erected in a public place by Aemilius Rufinus, a high-ranking official in Campania, during the reign of Theodosius and Valentinian (late 4th century CE).

EDCS-12401072.translation: To our lord Valentinian, parent of the state, pious, fortunate, victorious, ever Augustus. Naeratius Scopius, a man of great renown, consular of Campania, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-12401072.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Naeratius Scopius, a consular of Campania, to the emperor Valentinian (likely Valentinian III, 5th century CE).

EDCS-12401073.translation: To Afinia Calliste, daughter of Numerius, daughter of Caius.
EDCS-12401073.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Afinia Calliste, daughter of Numerius and granddaughter of Caius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401074.translation: To Anicius Bassus, Anicius Aucenius Bassus, a man of great renown, proconsul of Campania, judging with sacred authority, restorer of the fatherland since its very origin, a patron to all in common. The Beneventan people.
EDCS-12401074.summary: This inscription honors Anicius Bassus, a former proconsul of Campania, recognizing his patronage and service to the city of Beneventum.

EDCS-12401075.translation: To Anicius Bassus, a man of great renown, proconsul of Campania, judging with sacred authority, most outstanding patron and unique in all things. The Esquilina region placed this outstanding and special gift beyond other honors, mindful of all rightly done.
EDCS-12401075.summary: This inscription commemorates the honors bestowed upon Anicius Bassus, a proconsul of Campania, by the Esquilina region for his distinguished service.

EDCS-12401076.translation: To Betitia Plotina, daughter of Caius P.
EDCS-12401076.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Betitia Plotina, daughter of Caius P., likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401083.translation: Clodius Octavianus, a man of great renown, gave from his own adornment.
EDCS-12401083.summary: This inscription records a dedication of an object or adornment by Clodius Octavianus, a distinguished individual.

EDCS-12401084.translation: To Egnatia Certiana, a most distinguished woman, daughter of Caius Egnatius Certus, the consul, a most outstanding patroness. Her parasites.
EDCS-12401084.summary: This inscription honors Egnatia Certiana, daughter of a consul and a patroness, dedicated by her 'parasites' (clients or companions).

EDCS-12401085.translation: To Marcus Flavius Cornelius Marcellinus, of consular rank... [to] the forum of Beneventum... for his outstanding care and love. The Beneventan people.
