EDCS-12401493.translation: To the Divine Manes of Tettia Primitiva.
EDCS-12401493.summary: A simple funerary dedication to a woman named Tettia Primitiva.

EDCS-12401494.translation: To Tigia, faithful freedwoman of Gnaeus.
EDCS-12401494.summary: A dedication to a freedwoman named Tigia.

EDCS-12401496.translation: To the Divine Manes: Quintus Titacius Ephebus [dedicated this] to his deserving wife and to Caius Trebonius Celer [dedicated this] to his deserving mother.
EDCS-12401496.summary: A joint funerary dedication by a husband to his wife and by a son to his mother.

EDCS-12401497.translation: Iucunda, freedwoman of a woman, made this for herself and for her husband, Rufus, son of Caius Faltonius Stellatinus, and for Saturninus.
EDCS-12401497.summary: A funerary dedication made by a freedwoman for herself, her husband, and another individual named Saturninus.

EDCS-12401498.translation: To the Divine Manes: Tituleia Rufina [dedicated this] to her most dutiful father, Quinto Tituleio Fido.
EDCS-12401498.summary: A funerary dedication by a daughter to her deceased father.

EDCS-12401499.translation: To the Divine Manes: Munatia Felicitas made this for her deserving husband, Quinto Tituleio Marcellino, who lived 45 years and with me for 18 years.
EDCS-12401499.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife to her deceased husband, specifying their ages and duration of marriage.

EDCS-12401500.translation: Tituleius Ursulus made this to the Divine Manes of Tituleia Carpeola.
EDCS-12401500.summary: A funerary dedication by a man named Tituleius Ursulus to Tituleia Carpeola.

EDCS-12401501.translation: Publius, son of Trebullius, lived 10 years and 1 month and 2 days, and ordered this to be made for himself.
EDCS-12401501.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy, Publius Trebullius, who died at a very young age.

EDCS-12401502.translation: To the Divine Manes: Trebellius Aurelius P., who lived 10 years and 3 months, [dedicated this] to MAT. To the Divine Manes. To the Divine Manes.
EDCS-12401502.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph for a young boy named Trebellius Aurelius P., possibly indicating multiple dedications.

EDCS-12401503.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lollia Euphrosine, mother, and his brother, made this for the well-deserving Lucius Trebonius Beneventanus, who lived 36 years, 8 months, and 5 days.
EDCS-12401503.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Lucius Trebonius Beneventanus by his mother and brother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401505.translation: To the memory of Marcus Trebulanus Gagilus Telesforianus, most unfortunate, most sweet, who lived 9 years, 6 months, and 12 days. Trebulanus Telesforus and Natria Gagilia, his parents.
EDCS-12401505.summary: A funerary epitaph for the young Marcus Trebulanus Gagilus Telesforianus, set up by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401506.summary: A fragmented inscription, likely the beginning of a name or dedication, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401507.translation: Turellia Felix, freedwoman of a woman, made this from her own property for herself and her own, and for Manius Caprelius, freedman of Manius, her husband, and for her son Marcus Turellius Auctus, son of Manius, while she was alive.
EDCS-12401507.summary: A funerary epitaph by a freedwoman, Turellia Felix, for herself and her family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401508.translation: Gnaeus Turpilius, son of Caius, of Murra, son of Quintus; Decimus Vettius, son of Decimus; Galus; Clyte, freedwoman of Asvia, a woman [---]
EDCS-12401508.summary: A fragmentary inscription listing several Roman names, possibly related to a funerary monument or property ownership, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401509.translation: Turpilia Quarta, freedwoman of Caius, and Volumnia Iucunda, freedwoman of Quintus, her heirs, saw to the construction.
EDCS-12401509.summary: A funerary epitaph indicating that the heirs, Turpilia Quarta and Volumnia Iucunda, were responsible for the construction of the monument, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401511.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudia Maxima, mother, made this for Lucius Tursellius Priscus, who lived 13 years, 11 months.
EDCS-12401511.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Tursellius Priscus, made by his mother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401512.translation: Turselia Myrine, freedwoman of a woman, for herself and her own; in the front 12 feet, in the field 12 feet.
EDCS-12401512.summary: An epitaph or boundary marker indicating land allocation for Turselia Myrine, a freedwoman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401513.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Crassicia Amanda for her husband Quintus Tussidius Primitivus, with whom she lived 8 years, 3 months, and for Tussidia IAIA [---]
EDCS-12401513.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Tussidius Primitivus and Tussidia IAIA, dedicated by Crassicia Amanda, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401514.translation: Valerius Fl[---] for Cebetina, his most holy daughter [---]
EDCS-12401514.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph dedicated by Valerius Fl[---] to his daughter Cebetina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401515.translation: Valerius Claudius, a veteran soldier, made this for his most worthy and faithful freedman Valerius Heliodorus, who lived 30 years, 7 months, and 11 days, a most deserving young man.
EDCS-12401515.summary: This funerary inscription records a tomb made by a veteran soldier for his deceased freedman, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401520.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Eytyxianus and Primila made this for their sweetest son Vassius Eutychianus, who lived 5 years, 9 months.
EDCS-12401520.summary: This funerary inscription records parents dedicating a tomb to their young deceased son, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401522.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Rutilia Arsinoe made this for her most devoted husband Titus Vatinus Secundus, who lived 37 years, 2 months, and 9 days.
EDCS-12401522.summary: This funerary inscription records a wife dedicating a tomb to her deceased husband, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401523.translation: Quintus Veidius, freedman of Quintus, [dedicated this] to Clodia.
EDCS-12401523.summary: This inscription records a dedication from a freedman to a woman, possibly a wife or patron, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401524.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Verecundus and Chrysis made this for their sweetest daughter Verecunda, who lived 9 years, 2 months.
EDCS-12401524.summary: This funerary inscription records parents dedicating a tomb to their young deceased daughter, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401525.translation: Lucius Vesonius Alexander for himself and for Cassia Prima, daughter of Marcus, for their deserving services.
EDCS-12401525.summary: This inscription records a man dedicating a burial plot for himself and his wife, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401526.translation: Attice, freedwoman of the woman, [dedicated this] in her own place and for Lucius Vesonius Ingenuus, her husband, 12 feet in front and 12 feet in the ground.
EDCS-12401526.summary: This inscription records a woman allocating burial space for herself and her husband, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401528.translation: Vesullia Hilara for herself and for her husband Gaius Pullius Pamphilus and for Vesullia Europa.
EDCS-12401528.summary: This inscription records a woman dedicating a burial plot for herself, her husband, and another individual, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401529.translation: Vibia Quieta made this for her well-deserving brother Decimus Vettius Primus.
EDCS-12401529.summary: This funerary inscription records a sister dedicating a tomb to her deceased brother, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401530.translation: Lucius Vibius, son of Publius, of the Stellatina tribe; Vibia Hilara, freedwoman of Lucius.
EDCS-12401530.summary: This inscription records the names and tribal affiliations of two individuals, likely for identification or as part of a dedication, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401532.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aulus Vibbius Optatus lived 11 years, 11 months, 19 days. His most unhappy parents made this.
EDCS-12401532.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of a young boy, Aulus Vibbius Optatus, made by his parents.

EDCS-12401533.summary: A fragmentary inscription that appears to name Vibia, possibly related to funerary dedications.

EDCS-12401534.translation: Vibia Chresta, freedwoman of Lucius, made this monument for herself and her kin, and for Gaius Rustius Thalassus, son of Lucius, and for Vibia Calybe, freedwoman of a freedwoman Lena, acquired with her own profit without harm to others; this monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-12401534.summary: A funerary monument set up by Vibia Chresta, a freedwoman, for herself, her family, and others, with specific conditions regarding inheritance.

EDCS-12401535.translation: Vibbia Cypris, daughter of Aulus, for herself and for Aulus Titrius Rufus, son of Aulus, her best husband.
EDCS-12401535.summary: An epitaph dedicating a monument by Vibbia Cypris to herself and her husband, Aulus Titrius Rufus.

EDCS-12401536.translation: Vibia Fausta for herself and for her husband Marius Lucceius.
EDCS-12401536.summary: A funerary dedication by Vibia Fausta for herself and her husband, Marius Lucceius.

EDCS-12401539.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. His sons made this for their very devoted mother, Viciria Procilla, daughter of Marcus.
EDCS-12401539.summary: A tombstone erected by sons for their devoted mother, Viciria Procilla.

EDCS-12401540.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Victor, husband, who lived 50 years, 7 days. Rufina to her husband, with whom she lived 20 years, 10 [months?]. To her most devoted husband.
EDCS-12401540.summary: A tombstone for Victor, set up by his wife Rufina, commemorating their marriage and his lifespan.

EDCS-12401542.translation: Gaius Umbrius, son of Caius, [from the tribe] Tacitia; Lucia, freedwoman of Lucius; Phi; Gaius Umbrius, son of Titus.
EDCS-12401542.summary: A list of names, possibly related to a family or property ownership, with individuals identified by parentage and status.

EDCS-12401544.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Vibbia Marcella made this for her well-deserving husband, Gaius Umbrius.
EDCS-12401544.summary: A tombstone erected by Vibbia Marcella for her deserving husband, Gaius Umbrius.

EDCS-12401545.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Voconius Felicissimus, who lived 6 years, 2 months, 10 days. Voconius Felicissimus, his father [made this].
EDCS-12401545.summary: A tombstone recording the short life of Voconius Felicissimus, made by his father of the same name.

EDCS-12401547.translation: Zoticus leaves here his bare and empty name; his body has dissolved into ashes and his life into the ether.
EDCS-12401547.summary: This is an epitaph for Zoticus, a deceased individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401551.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly indicating ownership or a burial marker, naming Macer.

EDCS-12401566.translation: To Avilia Primitia, a most holy woman who lived 27 days, TET Fus us to his wife with whom he lived.
EDCS-12401566.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by TET Fus us to his wife, Avilia Primitia, who died at a very young age.

EDCS-12401574.translation: ysius for himself and for ae and for his freedman Marci ogeni.
EDCS-12401574.summary: This inscription seems to record the erection of a monument or a burial for ysias and his freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401575.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, possibly a funerary monument, lamenting an unhappy mother.

EDCS-12401578.translation: In memory of the Venerable, deposited in peace on the Nones of October, during the consulship of the most illustrious Illu viro.
EDCS-12401578.summary: This inscription marks a burial during the consulship of 'Illu viro', likely in the late Roman period, commemorating a person named Veneranda.

EDCS-12401579.translation: Here rests Mamercius Marcellinus, a praiseworthy man, a curator, who lived more or less 30 years, buried on the fourth day before the Nones of November, during the consulship of Flavius Symmachus and Boethius, most illustrious men.
EDCS-12401579.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Mamercius Marcellinus, a curator who died around age 30, with the burial date specified by the consulship of Flavius Symmachus and Boethius.

EDCS-12401580.translation: Here rests in peace Ampelius, who lived 10 years, in the sixth indiction, after the consulship of Basilius, a most illustrious man.
EDCS-12401580.summary: This funerary epitaph records the burial of Ampelius, who died at age 10, dated by the consulship of Basilius and a specific indiction.

EDCS-12401582.translation: Here rests in peace Felicitas, an honorable virgin, who lived more or less 6 years, 1 month, and 22 days, deposited on the fifth day before the Ides of February, 12 years after the consulship of Basilius, a most illustrious man, in the first indiction.
EDCS-12401582.summary: This funerary epitaph records the burial of Felicitas, an honorable virgin, who died at a young age, dated by the consulship of Basilius and a specific indiction.

EDCS-12401585.translation: Here rests Acholitus the elder, who lived years
EDCS-12401585.summary: This is an incomplete funerary epitaph for Acholitus the elder, indicating his name and the start of his lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12401586.translation: Here rests Faustinus the elder, who lived for [years].
EDCS-12401586.summary: This inscription records the death of Faustinus the elder, with a lacuna for his age.

EDCS-12401587.translation: Aurelius Munnus, who lived for 2 years, was buried on the fourth day before the Kalends of July, on a Wednesday.
EDCS-12401587.summary: This inscription records the burial of Aurelius Munnus, detailing his age and burial date.

EDCS-12401589.translation: Caius Discolius made this for his wife Helpidia, who lived with him without any dispute for 10 years, 9 months, and 13 days, to a well-deserving husband, the day before the Nones of August.
EDCS-12401589.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Caius Discolius for his wife Helpidia, specifying their years together and the date of erection.

EDCS-12401590.translation: Burial of Fortunus, who lived for 3 years, 4 months, and 17 days, in peace.
EDCS-12401590.summary: This inscription records the burial of Fortunus, providing his age and stating he died 'in pace'.

EDCS-12401592.translation: Memorial for three most innocent brothers, the eldest of whom was named A, lived for 10 years, 2 months, and 5 days. Modestia their mother made this for her sons.
EDCS-12401592.summary: This inscription is a memorial erected by Modestia for her three sons, detailing the age of the eldest.

EDCS-12401594.translation: For Gaius Aemilius, son of Caius, of the Menenia tribe, Sabinus, who lived for 18 years. Antonia Secunda, daughter of Publius, of the Menenia tribe, made this for her brother Publius Aprius, son of Publius, of the Menenia tribe, Celer.
EDCS-12401594.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication for Publius Aprius Celer, mentioning his age and relatives.

EDCS-12401596.translation: [Nnia] Felicitas made this for her well-deserving husband Aurelius Felix. SIQ VETDRF
EDCS-12401596.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication from Nnia Felicitas to her husband Aurelius Felix, with illegible text following.

EDCS-12401597.translation: DE SIA For Publius Publicius, father of Curtia Lucia, freedwoman Hilara. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-12401597.summary: This inscription establishes a perpetual burial plot for Publius Publicius, ensuring the monument does not pass to heirs.

EDCS-12401598.translation: For Publius Publicius Maximus, son of Publius, the haruspex. His mother made this.
EDCS-12401598.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by a mother for her son, Publius Publicius Maximus, who was a haruspex.

EDCS-12401599.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Statius Restutus made this for his dearest son, Lucius Statius Restutus.
EDCS-12401599.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication from Lucius Statius Restutus to his son, also named Lucius Statius Restutus.

EDCS-12401600.translation: To the memory of Trebatius Victorinus, who lived 35 months; Iaenia made this for him as one deserving well.
EDCS-12401600.summary: A funerary epitaph for Trebatius Victorinus, set up by Iaenia, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401601.translation: To the Divine Shades. Vettia Iuliane, his wife, and Gellia Iuliane, his daughter, made this for Gaius Gellius Germanus, a veteran of Syria, a most simple man, as one deserving well.
EDCS-12401601.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Gellius Germanus, set up by his wife and daughter, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401602.translation: Publius Sertorius Stellatina, son of Marcus, of the VI Legion, his brother Manius Sertorius Stellatina, son of Marcus, ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-12401602.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Sertorius Stellatina, set up by his brother, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401604.translation: By permission of the town councillors, the tribunal of Publius Camurius Gemellus.
EDCS-12401604.summary: An inscription recording the dedication of a tribunal under the authority of the town councillors, likely from a provincial town in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12401605.translation: Casineius Pudens made this for his wife Casinia Secunda, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-12401605.summary: A funerary epitaph for Casinia Secunda, set up by her husband, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401607.translation: Eggius Apollinaris [made this] for his wife Minia Felicissima, with whom he lived for 45 years.
EDCS-12401607.summary: A funerary epitaph for Minia Felicissima, set up by her husband, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401609.translation: To Hercules, his vow having been fulfilled, Gaius Ennius Primus.
EDCS-12401609.summary: A votive inscription to Hercules, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401611.translation: Lucius Turselius Restutus willingly paid his vow for the health and safe return of Lucius Turselius Maximus and Lucius Silvanus Casanicus.
EDCS-12401611.summary: A votive inscription expressing gratitude and relief for the safe return of Lucius Turselius Maximus and Lucius Silvanus Casanicus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401612.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Aper and Hatria Hesperis made this for their most sweet and most longed-for freedman Ianuarianus, who lived 12 years and 10 months, as one deserving well.
EDCS-12401612.summary: A funerary epitaph for the freedman Ianuarianus, set up by his patrons Aper and Hatria Hesperis, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401613.translation: To the Divine Shades. Minia Felix set up this monument.
EDCS-12401613.summary: A funerary epitaph for Minia Felix, set up by an unnamed individual, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401614.translation: To Menenia, freedwoman of Marcus Teidius, freedwoman of Numisius Publius, of the Menenia tribe.
EDCS-12401614.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Menenia, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401615.translation: To Potitus, son of his mother, who well deserved it, slave of Patulcia Tyche.
EDCS-12401615.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Potitus, a slave, probably from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401619.translation: Of Quintus Aqueius, son of Quintus, of the Bur tribe.
EDCS-12401619.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment for Quintus Aqueius, likely from the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-12401620.translation: Sacred to Hercules. Quintus Fuficius Modestus, from the VI legion, [dedicated this].
EDCS-12401620.summary: This is a dedication to Hercules by Quintus Fuficius Modestus, a soldier from the VI legion, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12401624.translation: To Silvanus of Caesarianensis. Trophimus, the manager, by a vow.
EDCS-12401624.summary: This is a votive dedication to Silvanus of Caesarianensis by Trophimus, an estate manager, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12401625.translation: Gaius Clodius Pius, son of Publius, of the Stellatina tribe, of the 30th legion: While I lived, I lived as befits a free man; only what I have eaten and drunk is mine.
EDCS-12401625.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Clodius Pius, a soldier of the 30th legion, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE, expressing a materialistic philosophy of life.

EDCS-12401629.translation: Quintus Vinius, son of Quintus, of the Galeria tribe, twice a magistrate with judicial power, to his sister Vinia, daughter of Quintus, Tertia, daughter of Publius, his mother, and to his son.
EDCS-12401629.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for multiple family members, including Quintus Vinius, a local magistrate, likely from the late 1st century BCE or early 1st century CE.

EDCS-12401633.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus, the greatest, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, acclaimed imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of Emperor Severus Augustus, holding tribunician power, proconsul, rebuilt the bridge, which had fallen into ruin from age, from the ground up with their own money.
EDCS-12401633.summary: This is an official inscription recording the reconstruction of a bridge by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely around 202-209 CE.

EDCS-12401634.translation: To the divine Fortune of Folianensis. Liberalis Umbrius Polytimus, the manager, gave this altar and house in fulfillment of a vow, [dedicated] to you, Verzobius, and to all yours.
EDCS-12401634.summary: This is a votive dedication of an altar and house to the local goddess Fortuna by an estate manager, Liberalis Umbrius Polytimus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12401635.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Titus Vatinius Vatinianus and Titus Vatinius Vitalis, sons of Vatinianus, gladly paid their vow.
EDCS-12401635.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titus Vatinius Vatinianus and his son Titus Vatinius Vitalis, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-12401637.translation: Dedicated on the 13th day before the Kalends of January [during the consulship] of our Emperors Gallus Augustus for the second time and Volusianus Augustus. To Silvanus, the most outstanding Publicensis [restored name] to [restored name] and [restored name].
EDCS-12401637.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus, made during the consulship of Gallus and Volusianus (251-252 AD), possibly by an official or a group of citizens from Publici.

EDCS-12401639.translation: I, Gaius Acellius Syneros, freedman of Gaius and Lucius, a pork merchant, [made this] for myself and for my wife Calpurnia Phyllis, daughter of Spurius, and for Gaius Acellius Falernus, son of Gaius, former aedile, praetor, duovir for five years, prefect of the engineers. Young man, desist and contemplate my tomb; I have made it so that I might have something to use. I have wronged no one; I have done many good deeds. Live well; hurry. This is where you must come.
EDCS-12401639.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Gaius Acellius Syneros, a freedman and merchant, for himself and his family, reflecting on his life and urging the passerby to appreciate his tomb.

EDCS-12401640.translation: Gratus and Sabinus, freedmen of Faustus the Augustalis, made [this].
EDCS-12401640.summary: This is a short dedication or record of a commission, likely for a monument or artwork, made by two freedmen named Gratus and Sabinus, associated with Faustus Augustalis.

EDCS-12401641.translation: To the Divine Shades. Petronius Primigenianus [dedicated this] to his wife Aerullia Iusta.
EDCS-12401641.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Petronius Primigenianus to his deceased wife, Aerullia Iusta.

EDCS-12401643.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caius Umbrius Liberalis made [this] for Hyalissus, his well-deserving patron.
EDCS-12401643.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Caius Umbrius Liberalis to his patron, Hyalissus.

EDCS-12401644.translation: To the Divine Shades. Libonius Primianus made [this] for his wife Umbria Secundina, with whom he lived for 35 years, who deserved well.
EDCS-12401644.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Libonius Primianus to his wife, Umbria Secundina, noting their 35 years of marriage.

EDCS-12401645.translation: To the Divine Shades. His sons and grandsons made [this] for Lucius Luccaeus Severinus, son of Quintus. His wife Sextidia Albana, with whom he lived for 40 years, [also contributed/made it].
EDCS-12401645.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Lucius Luccaeus Severinus, made by his children, grandchildren, and wife, noting 40 years of marriage.

EDCS-12401646.translation: Marcus Munatius, son of Marcus, son.
EDCS-12401646.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmentary or abbreviated genealogical record or a very simple funerary marker for Marcus Munatius.

EDCS-12401647.translation: To the Divine Shades. Petronia Prima, her mother, made [this] for her most longed-for daughter, Petronia Brisidia.
EDCS-12401647.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Petronia Prima to her beloved daughter, Petronia Brisidia.

EDCS-12401649.translation: To Ibuleia, daughter of Marcus, his mother. To Publius Sertorius, son of Publius, his father.
EDCS-12401649.summary: This inscription appears to record the dedication of a tomb or monument by a mother to her daughter, Ibuleia, and by implication, by children to their father, Publius Sertorius.

EDCS-12401651.translation: Felicia, unhappy, saw to its erection.
EDCS-12401651.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Felicia arranged for the erection of a monument, likely for herself or a relative.

EDCS-12401665.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Publicius Priscinus made this for his most beloved wife Ameria Ianuaria, who deserved well, and for himself while alive.
EDCS-12401665.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication of a tomb by Quintus Publicius Priscinus for his wife Ameria Ianuaria and himself.

EDCS-12401669.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Xardanus made a monument for himself.

EDCS-12401672.translation: Rufina rests here in the sleep of peace, who lived for more or less 6 years; she was placed to rest on the Ides of August, in the 14th indiction, in the 24th year after the consulship of the most distinguished Basilius.
EDCS-12401672.summary: This funerary inscription records the death and burial of Rufina, a child who lived approximately 6 years, with a precise dating based on imperial consulates and indictions, likely from the 5th or 6th century.

EDCS-12401673.translation: To Lord Justinus, perpetual Augustus, in his 4th year and also his second consulship, in the 3rd indiction.
EDCS-12401673.summary: This is a dating formula referring to the reign of Emperor Justin I, likely associated with an official inscription or document.

EDCS-12401674.translation: Sacred to Fontus. Quintus Pomponius Rufus, priest.
EDCS-12401674.summary: This is a dedication to the deity Fontus by Quintus Pomponius Rufus, who held the priesthood.

EDCS-12401677.translation: Sextus Pompeius Moderatus wished to dedicate an altar to the lord Silvanus for the health of himself and his family, in thanks for keeping him and his family safe; he paid his vow and dedicated the altar.
EDCS-12401677.summary: This votive inscription records Sextus Pompeius Moderatus dedicating an altar to the god Silvanus to give thanks for his family's safety.

EDCS-12401678.translation: To Iulia Augusta, mother of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, mother of the Emperor and of the camp, the colony Iulia Concordia Augusta Felix Beneventum, devoted to the majesty of the Augusti, in its territory which also encircles the land of the Caudini, up to the wall.
EDCS-12401678.summary: This is an honorific inscription by the colony of Beneventum dedicated to Iulia Domna, mother of Emperor Septimius Severus, affirming loyalty to the imperial family.

EDCS-12401680.translation: Sextus Aequanius, son of Sextus, of the Stellatina tribe, in the 30th legion.
EDCS-12401680.summary: This is a personal identification of Sextus Aequanius, son of Sextus, including his tribal affiliation and military service in the 30th legion.

EDCS-12401681.translation: Marcus Cluvius Priscus, son of Marcus, of the Falerna tribe, in the 3rd praetorian cohort, in the centuria of Priscus; he served for 10 years, he lived for 30 years.
EDCS-12401681.summary: This is a military epitaph for Marcus Cluvius Priscus, detailing his tribal origin, service in the praetorian guard, and his age at death and length of service.

EDCS-12401683.translation: To Gaius Valerius Marcellus, son of Gaius, a veteran soldier of the 11th Urban Cohort, a most excellent husband, for himself and his family, with a front of 12 feet and a depth of 12 feet.
EDCS-12401683.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a veteran soldier, specifying the dimensions of his plot.

EDCS-12401684.translation: Lucius Scribonius Libo, father, and Lucius Scribonius Libo, son, had these towers built at the expense of the patrons, by decree of the town councillors.
EDCS-12401684.summary: This inscription records the construction of towers by a father and son, acting as patrons, by official decree.

EDCS-12401685.translation: Lucius Scribonius Libo, father, and Lucius Scribonius Libo, son, had this tower built at the expense of the patrons, by decree of the town councillors.
EDCS-12401685.summary: This inscription records the construction of a tower by a father and son, acting as patrons, by official decree.

EDCS-12401686.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Scribonius, identified as a patron.

EDCS-12401688.summary: This inscription dedicates something to Marcus Sempronius Saturninus, who held the position of Augustalis.

EDCS-12401690.translation: To Thima, who lived 18 years. Marcus Sempronius Atticus to his most exceptional wife.
EDCS-12401690.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caecilia Thima, set up by her husband, Marcus Sempronius Atticus.

EDCS-12401691.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Catius Severus, son of Gaius, and Allidia Helpis to their most excellent son, Gaius Catius Felix, with a front of 12 feet and a depth of 12 feet.
EDCS-12401691.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Catius Felix, set up by his parents, specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-12401692.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Chrestus, for his well-deserving wife, Chresima.
EDCS-12401692.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Chresima, dedicated by her husband, Chrestus.

EDCS-12401693.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberius Claudius Restitutus for himself and Claudia Tertulla, his partner, and Claudius Felix, his son, and his family, with a front of 14 feet and a depth of 14 feet.
EDCS-12401693.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Restitutus and his family, specifying plot dimensions.

EDCS-12401694.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sextus Pompeius Domitius, son of Sextus, to Herennia Secundilla, daughter of Marcus, his most excellent wife.
EDCS-12401694.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Herennia Secundilla, dedicated by her husband, Sextus Pompeius Domitius.

EDCS-12401695.translation: Numerius Firveius, son of Numerius, from the Falernian tribe, and Fufia, daughter of Caius, wife of Numerius Firveius, son of Numerius, and Flaccus, son of Lucius Firveius, son of Numerius.
EDCS-12401695.summary: This inscription lists the names of family members, likely related to funerary monuments or property ownership.

EDCS-12401696.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Petronius Severus to his most beloved mother, Iunia Severa.
EDCS-12401696.summary: This is a funerary dedication from a son to his mother, Iunia Severa.

EDCS-12401697.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Narcissus made this for his daughter Felicitas, who lived two years, six months, fifteen days, and eleven hours.
EDCS-12401697.summary: This funerary inscription records the premature death of a young girl, Felicitas, dedicated by her father, Narcissus.

EDCS-12401698.translation: To Novius, his own... and his own family.
EDCS-12401698.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial for an individual named Novius and possibly his family.

EDCS-12401700.translation: To the departed spirits. Herennia Priscilla to her most rare husband, Publius Petronius Severus.
EDCS-12401700.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication from a wife to her husband, Publius Petronius Severus.

EDCS-12401701.translation: To the departed spirits. Sextus Pompeius Domitus [made this] for his brother Sextus Pompeius Alximus, and for his son Sextus Pompeius Domitus, and for his wife Belleia Restituta.
EDCS-12401701.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Sextus Pompeius Domitus for his brother, son, and wife.

EDCS-12401702.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a ownership mark or part of a larger monument belonging to Quintus Pomponius Rufus.

EDCS-12401703.translation: Marcus Vibius Vernio, freedman of Marcus Lucius, made this for himself and for Pamphilus, freedman of Caius Aesius, and for Palladio, freedwoman of Lucius Aesius.
EDCS-12401703.summary: This inscription records a self-dedicated tomb or monument by Marcus Vibius Vernio, also including space for his freedmen and freedwomen.

EDCS-12700218.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 13th time,Imperator for the 6th time, Consul V, father of the country, made the road and bridges from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-12700218.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's construction of a road and bridges, funded by himself, likely commemorating a significant public works project.

EDCS-12800116.translation: The council and people of Canusium erected [this] to our lord Flavius Julianus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-12800116.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the council and people of Canusium to the noble Caesar Flavius Julianus, likely in the 4th century CE.

EDCS-12800119.translation: The most distinguished council of the Canusinian city decreed that a statue be erected to Cassius Ruferius, most illustrious man, consular governor of Apulia and Calabria, for his merits and for the organization with which he renewed the city in every respect.
EDCS-12800119.summary: This inscription records the decree by the council of Canusium to erect a statue for the distinguished governor Cassius Ruferius due to his administrative merits, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12800537.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Dasimius Paederotianus lived 26 years and 9 months; his heirs made this for him, a well-deserving person.
EDCS-12800537.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Dasimius Paederotianus, who died at the age of 26 years and 9 months, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-12800538.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a funerary epitaph listing several names, possibly family members.

EDCS-12800548.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Graecidius Vitalis for Clatia Fortunata, his deserving wife, with whom he lived 40 years.
EDCS-12800548.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Clatia Fortunata, wife of Publius Graecidius Vitalis, with whom he had lived for 40 years.

EDCS-12800558.translation: Pusanio Liciniano, Pusania Epistole, 12 feet in front and 10 feet in the field.
EDCS-12800558.summary: This inscription seems to record land dimensions or boundaries related to Pusanio Liciniano and Pusania Epistole.

EDCS-12800560.translation: ...entarius was located. [---]ia, freedwoman of the Publii, Salvia, made [this] for herself and her family.
EDCS-12800560.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription records that Salvia, a freedwoman, commissioned a tomb for herself and her family.

EDCS-12800581.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription from Yncia to her dutiful daughter.

EDCS-12800597.translation: Romanus // August(us) // NINICA / remember the unjust / ade
EDCS-12800597.summary: This fragmented inscription, possibly a dedication or a personal note, mentions "Romanus", "Augustus", and a plea to remember the unjust.

EDCS-12800608.translation: CENT Communi s Sextu s Secundus
EDCS-12800608.summary: This inscription likely records the names Sextus Secundus and Communius, possibly related to a boundary marker or dedication.

EDCS-12800612.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Quintus Martius, likely a freedman, with significant portions missing.

EDCS-12800616.summary: This inscription records the name and title of Vibius Crispus, who held the office of curator, likely in an administrative or public role.

EDCS-12800618.translation: To Glapherai Menoglmen Arposenorheto, to the freedman of a woman, Lucius Numellius Chryserus, and to the freedman of a woman, Lucius Numellius Chryserus, and to Publius Minucius, freedman of Publius Platnus.
EDCS-12800618.summary: This inscription is a dedication or funerary text honoring multiple individuals, including Lucius Numellius Chryserus and Publius Minucius Platnus, who are identified as freedmen.

EDCS-12800767.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription that mentions individuals named Num[---] and Eroti, with the context and purpose of the inscription unclear.

EDCS-12800773.summary: This inscription records that Delphicus was the maker of the object or monument, indicating his role as a craftsman or commissioner.

EDCS-12800775.summary: This inscription likely indicates ownership or a relationship, naming Hermetis in connection with Tiberius Claudius Diadumenus, possibly as a freedman or property.

EDCS-12800790.summary: This inscription records that Lucius Attalus was the maker of the object or monument, indicating his role as a craftsman or commissioner.

EDCS-12800791.summary: This inscription simply records the name Lucius Avillius, suggesting it could be an owner's mark, a dedication, or part of a larger inscription.

EDCS-12800797.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or a record of an individual, Marcus Herennius, who is identified as being from Picentia.

EDCS-12800799.summary: This inscription simply records the name Publius Libuscidius, suggesting it could be an owner's mark, a dedication, or part of a larger inscription.

EDCS-12800800.translation: I am the slave of Marcus Betilienus.
EDCS-12800800.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a slave named Marcus Betilienus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800802.summary: This inscription is a name, likely an epitaph or a dedication by Marcus Valerius Felix, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800812.summary: This inscription records the name Caius Graecidius with an incomplete cognomen, likely an epitaph or a dedication from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800814.summary: This inscription is a name, likely an epitaph or a dedication by Publius Utinius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800818.summary: This inscription is a name, likely an epitaph or a dedication by Aufidius Fronimus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800819.summary: This inscription is a name, likely an epitaph or a dedication by Tiberius Iulius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800834.summary: This inscription is a name, likely an epitaph or a dedication by Lucius Rullius Lusimachus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800835.summary: This inscription records a slave named Servius Rullius, belonging to a certain Caius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800836.summary: This inscription is a name, likely an epitaph or a dedication by Alaucus Rullius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800841.summary: This inscription is a name, likely an epitaph or a dedication by Marcus Perennius Tigranus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12800844.summary: This inscription is a name, Sextus Murrius Festus, likely part of a funerary monument or a personal dedication.

EDCS-12800870.summary: This inscription consists of two Roman names, Lucius Gellius and Lucius Sempronius, possibly indicating a shared monument or a list of individuals.

EDCS-12800871.summary: This inscription presents a Roman name, Marcus Gratidius Menodorus, likely representing an individual on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-12800874.summary: This inscription is the name Publius Hertorius, which could be a personal monument or a dedication.

EDCS-12800882.summary: This inscription simply states the name Sextus Petronius, probably for a tomb or a personal monument.

EDCS-12800883.summary: This inscription simply states the name Sextus Petronius, probably for a tomb or a personal monument.

EDCS-12800884.summary: This inscription records the name Laetus Plaetorius, likely associated with a funerary context or a personal dedication.

EDCS-12800885.summary: This inscription contains the name Primus Plaetorius, suggesting a personal monument or funerary marker.

EDCS-12800886.summary: This inscription bears the name Pudens Plaetorius, likely for a funerary monument or a personal dedication.

EDCS-13900310.translation: The IIII viri [local officials] arranged for its repair and approved it, in accordance with the decree of the Senate.
EDCS-13900310.summary: This inscription records the repair of a public building or structure by four local officials, acting on a senatorial decree, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-13900317.summary: This inscription records the name and parentage of an individual, likely a tombstone inscription.

EDCS-13900338.translation: Caius Quinctius Hiero, freedman of Caius, and Publius Maius Hermogenes, freedman of Publius, and Decimus Arellius Salvius, freedman of Caius, and Caius Mettius, freedman of Caius, and Quintus Marius Philomusus, freedman of Quintus, bought three shops, an entrance hall, and a bronze doorway from the Mercuriales guild, with the approval of the Senate, for the revenue of the people.
EDCS-13900338.summary: This inscription details the purchase of commercial properties by several individuals, likely freedmen, for public revenue, indicating a business transaction from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-14804843.translation: Publius Octavius Tertius, his wife Attia Prima, freedwoman of a woman, and Attia Cnidia, freedwoman of Quintus, this monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-14804843.summary: This is a funerary inscription from the Roman Empire, recording the names of the deceased and stating that the tomb is not hereditary.

EDCS-16200944.translation: To Drusus Caesar, son of Tiberius Augustus, grandson of the deified Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Julius, twice consul, holding tribunician power for the second time.
EDCS-16200944.summary: This is a dedication, likely to a member of the Julio-Claudian imperial family, possibly on a public monument or statue, from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-17500021.summary: This inscription appears to be a simple ownership marker or a name on a monument, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500022.summary: This inscription likely records the names of two individuals, perhaps as dedicants, owners, or artisans, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-17500024.summary: This inscription is a genitive case indicating possession or belonging, likely referring to a tomb or a dedication made by Lollia Ithaca.

EDCS-17500026.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Julianus Augustus, Pious, Happy, Invincible, good for the state, born.
EDCS-17500026.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to the emperor Julian (Flavius Claudius Julianus), praising his virtues and rule, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-17500028.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajanus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, built the road from Benevento to Brundisium with his own money.
EDCS-17500028.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan's construction of a major road, funded personally, likely from the Roman Imperial period (c. 113-114 AD).

EDCS-17500029.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17500029.summary: An inscription recording Emperor Trajan's personal funding of a road construction project between Beneventum and Brundisium.

EDCS-17500031.translation: To our lords, Theodosius, Arcadius, and Honorius.
EDCS-17500031.summary: A dedication to the emperors Theodosius, Arcadius, and Honorius, likely a public monument base.

EDCS-17500032.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17500032.summary: An inscription recording Emperor Trajan's personal funding of a road construction project between Beneventum and Brundisium.

EDCS-17500033.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17500033.summary: An inscription recording Emperor Trajan's personal funding of a road construction project between Beneventum and Brundisium.

EDCS-17500034.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Traianus Hadrianus; 15,270 Roman miles of road, lost through age, with an additional 11,500,000 sesterces, the owners of the fields contributed; made.
EDCS-17500034.summary: An inscription detailing Emperor Hadrian's repair and extension of a road over 15,000 Roman miles, funded partly by local landowners.

EDCS-17500041.summary: A fragmentary inscription simply naming Gaius Julius Natalis.

EDCS-17500048.summary: A fragmentary inscription naming Lucius Herennius, possibly indicating a dedication or ownership related to him.

EDCS-17500049.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17500049.summary: An inscription recording Emperor Trajan's personal funding of a road construction project between Beneventum and Brundisium.

EDCS-17500050.translation: Of Heliodorus, son of Marcus, and Z[---?].
EDCS-17500050.summary: A fragmentary inscription possibly recording the names of Heliodorus and another individual, perhaps for a dedication or tomb.

EDCS-17500396.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Popphaedius Synhaemus.

EDCS-17500399.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Gelasinus.

EDCS-17500400.translation: Of the slave treasurer of the colony of Albani Aelia Augusta Aeclanum.
EDCS-17500400.summary: This inscription records the treasurer of the colony of Albani Aelia Augusta Aeclanum.

EDCS-17500403.summary: This incomplete inscription likely records Marcus Badius Anti[---], possibly a funerary or dedication text.

EDCS-17500406.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Claudia Maxima.

EDCS-17500407.translation: To Coelia Paula, daughter of Lucius, from B. C. [perhaps 'beneficio consulis' or similar].
EDCS-17500407.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Coelia Paula, daughter of Lucius.

EDCS-17500409.translation: Of Fructus, Decimus Titinius Liberalis, the manager.
EDCS-17500409.summary: This inscription records Fructus, identified as the manager of Decimus Titinius Liberalis.

EDCS-17500410.translation: Of Firminus, son of the Umbrians, Publius E. M. [perhaps 'eques magnificentissimus' or similar].
EDCS-17500410.summary: This inscription mentions Firminus, son of the Umbrians, with an honorific designation for Publius E. M.

EDCS-17500411.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Florius Chresimus.

EDCS-17500413.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Gellius Philodamus.

EDCS-17500414.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Caius Magius Vitalis.

EDCS-17500415.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Lucius Nasellius Primigenius.

EDCS-17500418.translation: From the workshop of Mani[us?] C. Sa[binius?] O. figlenc(ius?) Caius Am[---]
EDCS-17500418.summary: This inscription likely records ownership or origin from a workshop, possibly belonging to Mani[us?] C. Sabinius O. figlencius, with Caius Am[---] also mentioned, probably related to the workshop's operation or products.

EDCS-17500421.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Caius Ceionius Maximus.

EDCS-17500424.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Gnaeus Ateius Eros.

EDCS-17600274.summary: This inscription records the name and status of Metr, a slave, possibly on a funerary monument.

EDCS-17600279.summary: This inscription records the name of Lucius Cornelius, including his filiation, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-17600288.summary: This inscription records the name of Quintus Aclenius, including his filiation, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-17600291.summary: This inscription records the name and title of Utersinius, perhaps indicating his leadership role.

EDCS-17600298.summary: This inscription records the name of Quintus Hostrius Silvinus, likely on a funerary or dedicatory monument.

EDCS-17600321.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurellius Antoninus Pius Augustus Felix, greatest conqueror of the Parthians, greatest conqueror of the Germans, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 17th time, acclaimed imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, restored the collapsed fortifications of the river channel with their substructure and replaced the falling embankment of the river's flow with their protective works.
EDCS-17600321.summary: This inscription records public works undertaken by Emperor Marcus Aurelius, likely around his 17th year of tribunician power (c. 178 AD), demonstrating his commitment to infrastructure.

EDCS-17600322.translation: The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, acclaimed imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road and bridges from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17600322.summary: This inscription records the construction of a major road and bridges by Emperor Trajan, likely around his 13th year of tribunician power (c. 109 AD), financed by the emperor himself.

EDCS-17600324.translation: The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, acclaimed imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17600324.summary: This inscription records the construction of a major road by Emperor Trajan, likely around his 13th year of tribunician power (c. 109 AD), financed by the emperor himself.

EDCS-17600335.translation: To our lords, our Flavians, Theodosius, Arcadius, and Honorius, born for the good of the republic.
EDCS-17600335.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Theodosius, Arcadius, and Honorius, likely from the late 4th or early 5th century CE.

EDCS-17600339.translation: To the 113th [pillar/marker]. The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17600339.summary: This is a milestone inscription by Emperor Trajan, dating to his 13th tribunician power (110-111 CE), recording the construction of a road.

EDCS-17600341.translation: To the 109th [pillar/marker]. The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17600341.summary: This is a milestone inscription by Emperor Trajan, dating to his 13th tribunician power (110-111 CE), recording the construction of a road.

EDCS-17600356.translation: To the 110th [pillar/marker]. The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17600356.summary: This is a milestone inscription by Emperor Trajan, dating to his 13th tribunician power (110-111 CE), recording the construction of a road.

EDCS-17600357.translation: To the 91st [pillar/marker]. The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17600357.summary: This is a milestone inscription by Emperor Trajan, dating to his 13th tribunician power (110-111 CE), recording the construction of a road.

EDCS-17600358.translation: To the 92nd [pillar/marker]. The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17600358.summary: This is a milestone inscription by Emperor Trajan, dating to his 13th tribunician power (110-111 CE), recording the construction of a road.

EDCS-17600360.translation: The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17600360.summary: This is a milestone inscription by Emperor Trajan, dating to his 13th tribunician power (110-111 CE), recording the construction of a road.

EDCS-17600361.translation: To the 37th [pillar/marker]. The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17600361.summary: This is a milestone inscription by Emperor Trajan, dating to his 13th tribunician power (110-111 CE), recording the construction of a road.

EDCS-17600363.translation: To our lords Diocletian and Maximian, the Invincible, and Constantius and Maximian, Caesars.
EDCS-17600363.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Maximian, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-17600364.translation: To our lord Jovian, Augustus, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible, born for the good of the republic.
EDCS-17600364.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Jovian, who reigned from 363-364 CE, as a benevolent ruler.

EDCS-17700694.summary: This inscription records the names Bromius and Menodotus, possibly indicating ownership or a dedication, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-17700698.summary: This inscription appears to be a genitive dedication, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Publius Opicius Anthimus, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-17700699.summary: This inscription states that Gnaeus Pollius made this object or monument, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-17700711.summary: This inscription is a genitive dedication, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Lucius Axius Felix, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-17700715.summary: This inscription is a genitive dedication, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Decimus Fortunatus, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-17700719.translation: Epaphroditus, the agent of Aemilia Caricla, daughter of Caius
EDCS-17700719.summary: This inscription identifies Epaphroditus as the agent or freedman of Aemilia Caricla, daughter of Caius, likely a funerary or dedicatory text from the Imperial period.

EDCS-17700723.summary: This inscription is a genitive dedication, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Lucius Negilius Dionysus, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-17700741.summary: This inscription is a genitive dedication, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Lucius Titius Felix, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-17700742.summary: This inscription is a genitive dedication, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Pierius Cornelius Cethegus, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-17700746.summary: This inscription is a genitive dedication, likely for a tomb or monument, belonging to Publius Silius Atticus, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-17700760.summary: This is a personal funerary inscription identifying an individual by his praenomen and gentilicion, and his father's praenomen.

EDCS-17700761.summary: This is a personal funerary inscription identifying an individual by his praenomen and gentilicion, and his father's praenomen.

EDCS-17700795.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Julianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, 16 miles.
EDCS-17700795.summary: This inscription likely marks a distance of 16 miles along a road dedicated to the emperor Julian, dating to his reign (360-363 AD).

EDCS-17700796.translation: To our lords Theodosius, Arcadius, and Honorius, good for the Republic.
EDCS-17700796.summary: This inscription honors the co-emperors Theodosius I, Arcadius, and Honorius, dating to their joint rule (393-402 AD).

EDCS-17700800.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, son of the deified Constantius Pius, born good for the Republic.
EDCS-17700800.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantine I, highlighting his divine ancestry and benevolent rule, dating to his reign (307-337 AD).

EDCS-17700802.translation: To the deified Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, consul for the third time, emperor for the ninth time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, liberator of the Roman people, 11 miles. To our lords Theodosius, Arcadius, and Honorius, born good for the Republic.
EDCS-17700802.summary: This inscription marks a distance of 11 miles and honors both Constantine I and the later emperors Theodosius I, Arcadius, and Honorius, suggesting a complex dating within the late 4th or early 5th century AD.

EDCS-17700803.translation: Imperator Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, emperor for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17700803.summary: This milestone records Emperor Trajan's construction of the Via Appia from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense, dating to approximately 108 AD.

EDCS-17700806.translation: Imperator Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time.
EDCS-17700806.summary: This inscription records key imperial titles of Emperor Trajan, including his consulship for the fifth time and tribunician power for the sixth, dating to approximately 103 AD.

EDCS-17700810.translation: Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul for the 2nd time, restored the Via Herculia to its former condition.
EDCS-17700810.summary: This inscription records Emperor Maxentius's restoration of the Via Herculia, dating to his reign (306-312 AD).

EDCS-17700811.translation: To the deified Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, consul for the third time, emperor for the ninth time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, liberator of the Roman people, 11 miles.
EDCS-17700811.summary: This inscription marks a distance of 11 miles and honors Emperor Constantine I with his full titles, dating to approximately 313 AD.

EDCS-17700813.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul for the second time, restored the road of Hercules to its former state.
EDCS-17700813.summary: This inscription records Emperor Maxentius restoring the Via Herculia, likely in the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-17800045.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the fatherland, made the road from Beneventum to Brundisium at his own expense.
EDCS-17800045.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan funding the construction of the road from Beneventum to Brundisium, likely in the early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17800048.translation: Pilotas, slave of Arrius Quitus Caius.
EDCS-17800048.summary: This inscription identifies a slave named Pilotas and his owner, Arrius Quitus Caius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18000278.translation: Quinta, may you live in Christ.
EDCS-18000278.summary: This is a Christian epitaph for Quinta, wishing her eternal life in Christ, likely from the later Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-18000280.summary: This is a simple naming inscription, likely an epitaph, for Lucius Ratellius Famola, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18000281.translation: Patscasia, may she live in God.
EDCS-18000281.summary: This is a Christian epitaph for Patscasia, wishing her eternal life in God, likely from the later Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-18200056.translation: Heraclas son of Heraclides of Alexandria lived 50 years, is buried here.
EDCS-18200056.summary: This is an epitaph for Heraclas of Alexandria, stating his name, parentage, place of origin, and age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200084.summary: This inscription identifies Numisia Melissa as a freedwoman of Caius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200106.translation: Here rests the mistress Anna, daughter of Rebbitis Iulius, aged 56 years. May the memory of the righteous bring blessings.
EDCS-18200106.summary: This is a bilingual (Aramaic and Latin) epitaph for Anna, daughter of Rebbitis Iulius, indicating her age and invoking a blessing for the righteous, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-18200112.translation: Here rests in a good memory Anatoli, son of Justus, who lived 40 years. May peace be in his rest. The memory of the righteous brings blessings.
EDCS-18200112.summary: This is a bilingual (Latin and Aramaic) epitaph for Anatoli, son of Justus, stating his age and invoking a blessing for peace and the righteous, likely from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-18200114.translation: Here rests the well-remembered Samuel, son of Silanus, with his father-in-law Ezihiel, who lived 82 years. May peace be upon their resting place. Amen.
EDCS-18200114.summary: This inscription is a bilingual (Punic and Latin) funerary text for Samuel and his father-in-law Ezihiel, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-18200118.translation: Titus Septumulenus, son of Titus, gladly gives this gift to Hercules, a tenth offered in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-18200118.summary: This is a dedication to Hercules by Titus Septumulenus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200139.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Velasius Primitivus lived 71 years; he is buried here. Velasius Gnus made this for his father, who deserved well of him.
EDCS-18200139.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Velasius Primitivus and is dedicated by his father, Velasius Gnus.

EDCS-18200141.summary: A very brief funerary inscription stating that Geminia Aucta is buried here.

EDCS-18200152.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Licinius Minilus lived 7 years and 6 months. Marcus Licinius Hermogenes and Licinia Charite, parents, [made this] for their most devoted and deserving son.
EDCS-18200152.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Licinius Minilus, made by his parents.

EDCS-18200153.translation: Iunia Fausta, freedwoman of Sextus, for herself and for Iunia Nobili, her freedwoman, and for Publius Libuscius Faustus, Roman knight(?).
EDCS-18200153.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iunia Fausta, intended for herself, her freedwoman, and possibly Publius Libuscius Faustus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200159.translation: After the consulship of our lord Arcadius Augustus and Flavius Rufinus, most excellent consuls, Brizinus Maior, procurator, was laid to rest, having died on the 9th day before the Kalends of June, in the second hour of the day, having lived approximately 47 years.
EDCS-18200159.summary: This inscription records the death and burial of Brizinus Maior, a procurator, and provides a precise date and his age.

EDCS-18200167.translation: Here lies Casta, a maiden of fourteen years. May her rest be in peace.
EDCS-18200167.summary: This is a Greek funerary inscription for a fourteen-year-old maiden named Casta.

EDCS-18200170.translation: Here lies Anastases, facing the sun.
EDCS-18200170.summary: This is a Greek inscription indicating the burial place of Anastases.

EDCS-18200171.translation: Tomb of Kallistos, the cleaner, head of the synagogue, 3 years and 3 months old. May his rest be in peace.
EDCS-18200171.summary: This is a Greek funerary inscription for a young child named Kallistos, who held a position in the synagogue.

EDCS-18200172.translation: Here lies Aneketos, son of Eros, grandson of Aneketos, 11 months old; his repose in peace.
EDCS-18200172.summary: This is a funerary epigram for a child named Aneketos, detailing his parentage and age, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-18200173.translation: Here lies Sebeira, daughter of Jacob, the teacher; her repose in peace.
EDCS-18200173.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sebeira, identifying her father and his profession as a teacher, likely from the Roman Near East.

EDCS-18200174.translation: Here lies Asther, daughter of Syriano, two years old.
EDCS-18200174.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a child named Asther, stating her father's name and her age at death.

EDCS-18200175.translation: Tomb of Joseph, son of Joseph, the synagogue ruler.
EDCS-18200175.summary: This inscription marks the tomb of Joseph, who held the important position of synagogue ruler, indicating a Jewish burial in the Roman period.

EDCS-18200176.translation: Tomb of Iusta, daughter of Samoel.
EDCS-18200176.summary: This is a funerary marker for Iusta, daughter of Samoel, likely a Jewish burial from the Roman era.

EDCS-18200185.translation: Here rests Benricianus with his grandson Benricianiu.
EDCS-18200185.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Benricianus and his grandson, also named Benricianus, suggesting a family burial plot.

EDCS-18200188.translation: Here lies Pretiosa, daughter of Bitius.
EDCS-18200188.summary: This funerary inscription identifies Pretiosa as the daughter of Bitius, marking her burial place.

EDCS-18200189.translation: Here rest Andronicus, 11 months old, and Rosa, 19 months old, children of Boni, grandchildren of Sebbetius, and great-grandchildren of Viti.
EDCS-18200189.summary: This epitaph commemorates two young children, Andronicus and Rosa, detailing their ages, parents, and multiple generations of ancestors.

EDCS-18200190.translation: Here rests Faustinus, father, grandson of Faustinus, father, son of Viti, the hieros archon.
EDCS-18200190.summary: This inscription details the lineage of Faustinus, highlighting his roles as father and grandson, and identifies his father as Viti, a hieros archon.

EDCS-18200191.translation: Here rests Osses with his wife Maria and his grandson Sarmates. Ioses was the son of Boninus and died approximately 36 years old, and his wife died at 60 years old, the grandson died at [age].
EDCS-18200191.summary: This epitaph records the burials of Osses, his wife Maria, and grandson Sarmates, also providing the ages at death for Osses, Maria, and Sarmates.

EDCS-18200192.translation: Here rest Asella and Sarra, daughters of Opillo and Catella. Sarra, wife of Hintiu, lived about 70 years. Asella, a virgin, lived about 34 years. [Hebrew text: peace upon her rest]
EDCS-18200192.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of two sisters, Asella and Sarra, with their ages and familial connections, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-18200193.translation: Here rests Gesua with his wife Agnella. Gesua was the son of Marcellus and Anna, grandson of the grandfathers of Marcellus, and died at about 60 years of age. Agnella, wife of Gesua, was the daughter of Jositis and Maria, and granddaughter of Sarmatas, grandfather of grandfathers, and died at about 44 years of age.
EDCS-18200193.summary: This epitaph commemorates Gesua and his wife Agnella, detailing their lineage and ages at death, likely from the Roman provincial period.

EDCS-18200196.translation: Here rests Catella, 9 years old, daughter of the grandfather of Sebbethus.
EDCS-18200196.summary: This inscription marks the burial of a young girl named Catella, providing her age and a paternal lineage reference.

EDCS-18200197.translation: Here rests Marcellus. He caused great grief to his parents.
EDCS-18200197.summary: This short epitaph records the death of Marcellus and the sorrow he caused his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18200200.translation: Here rests Alexandra, a noblewoman, who rested at about [---] years. [Hebrew text: peace]
EDCS-18200200.summary: This funerary inscription mentions Alexandra, a 'pateressa' (noblewoman), whose age at death is partially preserved.

EDCS-18200212.translation: Cadmus for himself and his family.
EDCS-18200212.summary: This is a dedicatory or funerary inscription by Cadmus for himself and his household, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-18200213.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Mammius Amandus made this for his most beloved and deserving wife, Coelia Aphrodisias.
EDCS-18200213.summary: This tombstone inscription is dedicated by Mammius Amandus to his wife Coelia Aphrodisias, typical of Roman funerary practices.

EDCS-18200214.translation: While alive, they made this for Gaius Gabbius Quintio, freedwoman of Caius Gabbius, and Sabella, freedwoman of Caius Gabbius Staphilenus, in front 10 feet, in the field 12 feet. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-18200214.summary: This inscription records the construction of a tomb by freedwomen for themselves and their patron, specifying dimensions and inheritance restrictions.

EDCS-18200215.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Irrius Caprius made this for his deserving wife Irria Januaria and his deserving mother Irria Capriola.
EDCS-18200215.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Irrius Caprius for his wife Irria Januaria and his mother Irria Capriola, a common Roman family memorial.

EDCS-18200216.translation: Licinia Aphelea, freedwoman of Lucius, made this while alive for herself, and for her patron Lucius Licinius, son of Marcus, and for the freedmen sons: the freedman of Numerius Varienus and the freedman of Decimus Melior. The monument will not pass to an heir, except for those written above.
EDCS-18200216.summary: This epitaph records a freedwoman, Licinia Aphelea, who built a monument for herself, her patron, and other freedmen, with specific inheritance clauses.

EDCS-18200217.translation: Lucius Aquinius Faustus made this for his wife, Licinia Sabella, freedwoman of Caius, during his lifetime.
EDCS-18200217.summary: A funerary monument set up by Lucius Aquinius Faustus for his wife, Licinia Sabella, during his lifetime.

EDCS-18200218.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Publius Numisius Gallus set this up for his well-deserving grandmother, Tycheni, freedwoman of Papia Mani.
EDCS-18200218.summary: A funerary dedication by Publius Numisius Gallus to his grandmother, Tycheni, a freedwoman.

EDCS-18200219.translation: Marcus Pedius Lucrio, freedman of Marcus Pedius, during his lifetime made this monument for himself, and for his son Marcus Pedius Grato, freedman of Marcus Pedius, and for Pedia Hilarae; he will not be subject to an heir.
EDCS-18200219.summary: A funerary monument set up by Marcus Pedius Lucrio for himself, his son, and Pedia Hilarae, with a clause about inheritance.

EDCS-18200220.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Staia Hilaritas set this up for her dearest son, Staio Maximiano, who lived 18 years.
EDCS-18200220.summary: A funerary dedication by Staia Hilaritas to her son, Staio Maximiano.

EDCS-18200222.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Nicephor, freedman of a woman named Media, and Cleopatra, freedwoman of a woman named Media, mother, made this for themselves while alive.
EDCS-18200222.summary: A funerary monument set up by Nicephor and Cleopatra, freedpersons of a woman named Media, for themselves.

EDCS-18200223.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Lucius Raius Maximus, Ennia Flaminina, and Umettia Severa set this up for their well-deserving sister Raia Successae and mother.
EDCS-18200223.summary: A funerary dedication by Lucius Raius Maximus, Ennia Flaminina, and Umettia Severa to their sister Raia Successae and mother.

EDCS-18200226.translation: Marcus Lucceius, son of Caius, a commissioner for the second rank, supervised the cleaning of the swimming pool and the construction of the armor plating, from the urban works.
EDCS-18200226.summary: A record of public works completed under the supervision of Marcus Lucceius, a local magistrate.

EDCS-18200228.translation: Lucius Percennius Epicadus, freedman of Lucius, an Augustalis, paved the street for 800 feet.
EDCS-18200228.summary: A record of paving work done by Lucius Percennius Epicadus, a freedman and member of the Augustales, on a street.

EDCS-18200231.translation: For Balbus, father: 15 feet in front, 20 feet in the land.
EDCS-18200231.summary: A land measurement or boundary marker for a plot belonging to someone named Balbus.

EDCS-18200232.translation: For Caius Britti Philo, freedman of Caius: 12 feet in front, 15 feet in the land.
EDCS-18200232.summary: A land measurement or boundary marker for a plot belonging to Caius Britti Philo, a freedman.

EDCS-18200233.translation: Deccia, daughter of Marcus, Rufa, lies here.
EDCS-18200233.summary: This is a tombstone inscription identifying Deccia Rufa, daughter of Marcus, as the deceased.

EDCS-18200234.translation: Of Lucius Licinius Marci, son of Gallus, of the Galeria tribe: 12 feet in front, 15 feet in the land.
EDCS-18200234.summary: This inscription likely marks a boundary or a plot of land belonging to Lucius Licinius Marci, specifying its dimensions.

EDCS-18200235.translation: Here lies Quintus Tattius, son of Publius, of the Sergia tribe: 12 feet in the land.
EDCS-18200235.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Tattius, son of Publius, indicating his burial place and the size of his plot.

EDCS-18200236.summary: This inscription identifies Vibia, a freedwoman belonging to an unnamed female patron.

EDCS-18200244.translation: Of Silvana, daughter of Gnaeus Pomponius.
EDCS-18200244.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely part of a larger dedication or tombstone, honoring Silvana, daughter of Gnaeus Pomponius.

EDCS-18200250.translation: Satrius Storax and Liccaeus made the bones of Dionysia, [their] wife, here.
EDCS-18200250.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Satrius Storax and Liccaeus erected this monument for their wife/companion, Dionysia.

EDCS-18200261.translation: Here lies Hermas. In his early youth, while desiring to fix various images in the museum, he fell from on high. And Hermas is now confined by this weight; he left eternal tears for Carpus, his parent, who lived 21 years, 9 months, and 20 days. Carpus, most unhappy father from the colony, made [this].
EDCS-18200261.summary: This is a tragic epitaph for Hermas, who died from a fall while pursuing artistic endeavors, with his father Carpus commissioning the monument.

EDCS-18200265.translation: Lucius Offellius, son of Caius, of the Voltinia tribe, while alive, arranged for this to be made for himself and his family.
EDCS-18200265.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Lucius Offellius, son of Caius, commissioned his own tomb in advance of his death.

EDCS-18200266.translation: To the Divine Shades. To Pontia Gratilla, most holy wife, Marcus Aurelius Diomedes, while alive, [dedicated this] for her, benevolently deserving.
EDCS-18200266.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcus Aurelius Diomedes to his wife, Pontia Gratilla, whom he commissioned while alive.

EDCS-18200267.translation: Lives Titus Rufius, son of Gnaeus, of the Stellatina tribe, [together] with Rutilia, daughter of Quintus, [his] wife, and for themselves: 12 feet in front, [and space] in the land.
EDCS-18200267.summary: This inscription likely marks a family burial plot for Titus Rufius, his wife Rutilia, and possibly other family members, specifying dimensions.

EDCS-18200268.translation: To Aysius for himself, and for me, and for Saturnina, the freedwoman of Marcus Hermogenes.
EDCS-18200268.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aysius, mentioning himself and his freedwoman Saturnina, and likely dedicated by Hermogenes.

EDCS-18200271.translation: To the dearest priest who lived 2 years, 2 months, and 2 days, Mys.
EDCS-18200271.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a priest named Mys, mentioning his age at death.

EDCS-18200273.translation: Syneros, slave of Postumius Lucius, son of Postumius Lucius; Auctus, slave of Taronius Lucius, his master, to the household gods.
EDCS-18200273.summary: This dedication to the household gods is made by slaves, indicating their loyalty to their masters and potentially their own social standing.
